proposals.title: Stabilizing and Characterizing Metastable Protonation Isomers of Carbaporphyrinoid Anion Receptors via Computational Screening and Directed Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The properties of porphyrinoids and expanded porphyrins, such as their anion binding affinity, redox potentials, and optical gaps, are critically dependent on their protonation state. However, conventional protonation states dominate in standard conditions, limiting access to potentially useful property regimes. Pre-2026 work on carbaporphyrins and anion receptors like calix[4]pyrroles demonstrates that subtle structural changes can dramatically alter anion binding and electronic structure, suggesting that non-standard protonation states could unlock new functions if stabilized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that non-aqueous environments or macrocyclic confinement can thermodynamically and kinetically stabilize non-standard protonation isomers of carbaporphyrinoids and related anion receptors, and that these metastable states will exhibit significantly enhanced or inverted properties, such as superior anion selectivity or tunable redox couples, compared to their ground-state counterparts.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput DFT calculations (using Gaussian 16 with ωB97X-D/6-311+G(d,p)) and ab initio molecular dynamics (CP2K) to screen a library of candidate molecules derived from Sessler's carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers and dipyrrolylquinoxalines. The workflow will identify structures where alternative protonation states are within ~10-15 kcal/mol of the ground state and predict their NMR/IR signatures and electrochemical properties. Guided synthesis will target these states using predicted stabilizing media (e.g., low-polarity solvents with tailored hydrogen-bond donors/acceptors). Characterization will utilize in situ UV-Vis-NIR and EPR spectroelectrochemistry and cryogenic IR spectroscopy to trap and validate the metastable species.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, computationally guided strategy for accessing and exploiting metastable protonation states in functional π-conjugated systems. It will deliver new, property-enhanced variants of carbaporphyrinoid anion receptors with documented anomalous binding or redox behavior, pushing the boundaries of supramolecular design. The project will also provide fundamental insights into the role of the chemical environment in controlling proton transfer equilibria.
proposals.novelty: While protonation effects are well-known, the deliberate computational prediction and subsequent synthetic stabilization of a targeted, metastable protonation isomer for property enhancement is a non-obvious and unexplored strategy in porphyrinoid chemistry. This moves beyond observing protonation equilibria to actively designing and trapping a desired, non-thermodynamic state.

proposals.title: Mapping Function to Cavity: A High-Throughput Screening Study of Expanded Carbaporphyrinoids as Anion Receptors and Catalysts
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins offer tunable cavities for guest binding and catalysis, but design has been largely intuition-driven, focusing on hexaphyrins as optimal. In materials science, optimal pore size is discovered empirically through combinatorial libraries, as seen in MOF and zeolite research. Applying this data-driven 'materials genome' philosophy to porphyrinoids could systematically reveal superior cavity sizes for specific functions, moving the field beyond serendipity.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal cavity size for anion binding or catalysis within expanded porphyrinoids is not universally six pyrroles but is application-dependent and can be discovered through systematic synthesis and screening of a structurally homologous series with incrementally varied ring sizes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a homologous library of N-confused carbaporphyrinoids and their dimers, systematically varying the macrocycle size from tetra- to octapyrrole units, leveraging Sessler's expertise in carbaporphyrin and dimer synthesis. Each compound will be screened via high-throughput UV-Vis/fluorescence titrations against a panel of anions (F-, Cl-, H2PO4-, HCO3-) to determine binding affinities and selectivity profiles. Concurrently, catalytic potential for simple transformations (e.g., cyclohexane oxidation) will be assessed for metalated derivatives. Data will be analyzed using multivariate analysis to correlate cavity size, electronic structure, and performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, empirical map linking expanded porphyrin cavity size to anion receptor efficacy and catalytic activity. It will establish a high-throughput screening framework for macrocyclic chemistry and likely identify non-intuitive, optimal ring sizes for specific targets, challenging the hexaphyrin-centric paradigm.
proposals.novelty: The approach transplants the high-throughput, combinatorial discovery mindset from porous materials to discrete macrocyclic synthesis, a strategy not yet applied to expanded porphyrinoids. It directly tests the assumed supremacy of the six-pyrrole cavity by generating comparative data across a continuous size range.

proposals.title: A Calibrated Multi-Metric Aromaticity Scale: Resolving the Electronic Structure of Rubyrin and Complex Porphyrinoids
proposals.motivation: The aromaticity of expanded porphyrins like rubyrin remains ambiguous, as different experimental and computational metrics (NICS, ACID, HOMA, ring current) often yield conflicting classifications. This ambiguity hinders the rational design of porphyrinoids for applications in sensing, catalysis, and materials science. A unified, quantitative framework is urgently needed to definitively classify these systems and reconcile disparate indicators.
proposals.hypothesis: By benchmarking a suite of aromaticity metrics against a curated set of reference macrocycles with well-defined character, we can establish a calibrated, weighted scoring system. This system will provide a definitive and consistent classification of rubyrin's electronic nature, resolving current contradictions.
proposals.method: We will first synthesize and characterize a reference library of macrocycles (e.g., porphyrin [18-annulene], [26]hexaphyrin(1.1.1.1.1.1), and antiaromatic norcorrole). For each, we will perform integrated DFT calculations (B3LYP/6-311+G(d,p) level) to compute NICS(1)zz, HOMA, and ACID plots, alongside experimental NMR chemical shifts and X-ray structural analysis. Magnetically-induced current density (MICD) calculations will be performed using the GIMIC code. A principal component analysis (PCA) of this multi-metric dataset will generate a calibrated aromaticity scale, onto which rubyrin will be definitively mapped.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first calibrated, multi-metric aromaticity scale specifically for porphyrinoid macrocycles, resolving the long-standing ambiguity surrounding rubyrin. The resulting framework will serve as a general, transferable tool for the electronic classification of novel and complex porphyrinoid architectures, directly impacting their targeted design for anion sensing and other applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in measuring individual metrics, but in creating a statistically validated, weighted scoring system that resolves conflicts between them. This transforms a single-molecule question into a generalizable classification tool, moving beyond qualitative arguments to a quantitative standard.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Aromatic Character of Planar Rubyrin: A Combined Computational and Spectroscopic Study
proposals.motivation: Rubyrin, a hexaphyrin macrocycle, is a core expanded porphyrin with a planar, conjugated 26 π-electron framework. Its aromaticity is a subject of debate, as it lies at the boundary between Hückel and Möbius topologies, profoundly influencing its electronic structure, stability, and potential for applications in sensing and materials science. A quantitative, multi-faceted analysis of its aromatic character is essential to resolve this ambiguity and establish design principles for new functional macrocycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that planar rubyrin exhibits a significant, yet non-classical, global aromatic character, distinct from both simple porphyrins and twisted Möbius systems, which can be quantified through nucleus-independent chemical shift (NICS) calculations and manifested in distinct spectroscopic signatures.
proposals.method: Computational studies will employ density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP/6-31G(d) level to optimize the rubyrin structure and calculate NICS(0) and NICS(1)zz values on a grid within the macrocycle plane to map the induced ring current. Experimental validation will involve synthesizing the known rubyrin free base and its metal complexes (e.g., Ni(II), Cu(II)). Aromaticity will be probed via 1H NMR chemical shift analysis, particularly of the inner NH and β-pyrrolic protons, and by UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy to correlate the absorption profile with the degree of conjugation and electronic delocalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative map of aromaticity in planar rubyrin, directly linking computational indices to experimental observables. It will clarify the electronic nature of this benchmark expanded porphyrin, offering a validated model for predicting the properties of higher-order analogues and informing the design of macrocycles with tunable aromaticity for anion recognition and nonlinear optics.
proposals.novelty: While rubyrin's synthesis is known, a rigorous, multi-technique quantification of its aromaticity is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates established computational aromaticity probes with targeted synthesis and spectroscopic analysis from the Sessler group's expertise in anion-responsive porphyrinoids, applying a proven toolkit to an unresolved fundamental question in a key expanded porphyrin.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Supramolecular Recognition Dynamics of Large Anionic Guests and Fullerenes by Electronically-Tuned Rubyrin Macrocycles
proposals.motivation: While the Sessler group has pioneered the synthesis of expanded porphyrins like rubyrin and demonstrated their potential as large, electron-rich hosts, quantitative studies of their binding dynamics remain scarce. Understanding the kinetic and thermodynamic drivers for complexation with large, challenging guests like polyoxometalates and fullerenes is crucial for advancing applications in molecular sensing, sequestration, and supramolecular assembly, where both affinity and exchange rates are critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic electronic modulation of the rubyrin core, through the introduction of electron-donating or -withdrawing substituents, will provide a powerful handle to tune not only the binding affinity but also the association and dissociation kinetics for large anionic and neutral guests, establishing clear structure-dynamics relationships.
proposals.method: A series of rubyrin derivatives will be synthesized with varied substituents (e.g., -OMe, -F, -NO2) on the aryl rings to modulate the macrocycle's electron density and cavity character. Binding studies with model large anions (e.g., [PW12O40]3-, [Fe(CN)6]4-) and C60/C70 will be conducted using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) for ΔH, ΔS, and Ka. Stopped-flow UV-vis and variable-temperature 1H NMR spectroscopy will be employed to determine the association (k_on) and dissociation (k_off) rate constants, enabling a full thermodynamic and kinetic profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset linking rubyrin structure to the kinetics and thermodynamics of large guest binding. It will establish design principles for creating rubyrin hosts with tailored binding strength and exchange rates, directly informing their use in dynamic supramolecular systems and selective anion recognition platforms.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond the established qualitative host-guest chemistry of rubyrins to a quantitative, kinetics-focused investigation, uniquely targeting large anions and fullerenes simultaneously to uncover broader recognition principles not accessible with smaller guests like fluoride or chloride.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of a Core-Contracted, 24-π-Electron Rubyrin Analogue with a Carbaporphyrin-Derived Subunit
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins like rubyrin (a hexaphyrin) possess large, flexible cavities suitable for binding large anions but often suffer from conformational instability and weak binding affinities for smaller, harder anions like fluoride. Core-contracted porphyrinoids, which maintain heteroatom count but reduce macrocycle size, are underexplored and could enforce stronger, pre-organized binding pockets. This addresses a key challenge in anion receptor design: achieving high selectivity and affinity through cavity size and geometry control, a central theme in Sessler's pre-2026 work on anion-responsive macrocycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing one or more pyrrolic rings in a rubyrin framework with a contracted, aromatic subunit like a carbaporphyrin or a modified bipyrrole will create a stable, 24-π-electron porphyrinoid with a smaller, rigidified cavity. This structural inversion from expanded to contracted is predicted to dramatically enhance its affinity and selectivity for small, spherical anions like fluoride over larger halides.
proposals.method: The synthesis will proceed via a [3+1] or [4+2] MacDonald-type condensation, using a pre-formed, contracted dipyrromethene analogue derived from a known carbaporphyrin synthon (e.g., a modified 2,2'-bipyrrole or a tripyrrane with a carbocyclic ring) reacted with a complementary diformyl tripyrrane. The macrocycle will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Anion binding studies will be conducted using UV-vis-NIR and fluorescence titration in dichloromethane, with a focus on fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate, complemented by 1H NMR titrations to probe binding-induced conformational changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new class of 'contracted rubyrin' porphyrinoids, expanding the structural lexicon of core-modified macrocycles. It will establish a structure-function relationship demonstrating how core contraction, rather than expansion, can be leveraged for superior anion recognition. The resulting receptor may serve as a prototype for highly selective fluoride sensors or as a novel platform for studying anion-pi interactions in constrained environments.
proposals.novelty: While carbaporphyrins and rubyrins are known, the deliberate design of a rubyrin analogue with a *smaller* cavity while retaining its hexaphyrin-like heteroatom count is unprecedented. The concept of an 'inverse, contracted' structure directly inverts the prevailing expansion paradigm in porphyrinoid chemistry for anion recognition.

proposals.title: Anion Binding in Strapped Calix[4]pyrroles: Probing the Essential Role of the Core Through a Non-Pyrrolic Strap Design
proposals.motivation: Strapped calix[4]pyrroles, pioneered by Sessler and others, are potent anion receptors where a strap often incorporates pyrrolic NH donors to enhance binding affinity and selectivity. This design leaves a key question unanswered: is the strap's primary role to pre-organize the core and enforce a binding-competent conformation, or are its auxiliary NH groups essential for direct anion coordination? Resolving this is critical for the rational design of next-generation anion transporters and sensors, moving beyond empirical modifications to fundamental principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anion binding affinity and selectivity of a strapped calix[4]pyrrole can be retained, or strategically modulated, by replacing the traditional pyrrolic-NH-containing strap with a structurally analogous strap lacking hydrogen-bond donors, thereby demonstrating that the calix[4]pyrrole core is the primary and sufficient binding unit.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a strapped calix[4]pyrrole where the strap is a simple, rigid aromatic linker (e.g., a xylylene or diphenylacetylene bridge) connected via ether or alkyl linkages to the meso-positions, deliberately excluding any pyrrole, amide, or urea NH groups. This 'dumb strap' will be compared directly to a known NH-containing analogue (e.g., a dipyrromethane-based strap). Binding studies will be conducted via 1H NMR and UV-Vis titrations in dichloromethane with tetrabutylammonium salts of fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate. X-ray crystallography of host-guest complexes will be attempted to confirm binding mode.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively isolate the functional contribution of the calix[4]pyrrole core from that of the strap's auxiliary donors. It will provide a design principle for creating simplified, more synthetically accessible anion receptors and establish a benchmark for quantifying the energetic contribution of secondary interactions in complex receptor architectures.
proposals.novelty: While many studies add functional groups to straps to improve binding, systematically removing all H-bond donors from the strap to test the sufficiency of the core is a non-obvious, minimalist approach that challenges a prevailing design paradigm in anion receptor chemistry.

proposals.title: Quantifying Bicarbonate Affinity and Transport in a Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimer: An NMR and ITC Study
proposals.motivation: Bicarbonate (HCO3-) is a crucial biological anion involved in pH regulation and CO2 transport, yet its selective recognition and transport by synthetic receptors remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026 work by Sessler and others has established calix[4]pyrroles and related macrocycles as potent anion receptors, with recent studies highlighting chloride/bicarbonate antiport. However, quantitative binding data for HCO3- with novel, structurally complex receptors like carbaporphyrin dimers is scarce, limiting the design of next-generation anionophores.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unique, pre-organized cavity and expanded π-system of the recently reported Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer will exhibit a measurable and selective binding affinity for the bicarbonate anion, distinct from its affinity for smaller halides like fluoride and chloride.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the target Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer (Receptor 1). Binding studies will be conducted using 1H NMR titration techniques in dry, deuterated DMSO to determine association constants (Ka) by monitoring chemical shift changes of key pyrrolic NH or aromatic proton signals upon addition of tetrabutylammonium bicarbonate. Isothermal Titration Calmetry (ITC) will be performed in the same solvent to obtain complementary thermodynamic data (ΔH, ΔS, and Ka). Competitive extraction experiments from an aqueous phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) into a chloroform phase containing Receptor 1 will assess HCO3- transport efficiency relative to Cl-.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative binding and thermodynamic profile for bicarbonate with a carbaporphyrin-based dimeric architecture, directly testing its utility as a HCO3- receptor. The results will bridge the gap between the established anion-binding prowess of Sessler's macrocycles and the under-explored area of bicarbonate recognition, informing the future design of anionophores for physiological anion transport and sensing.
proposals.novelty: While anion binding to porphyrinoids is known, applying the recently developed, structurally innovative Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer specifically to the challenging bicarbonate anion is a novel and logical extension of Sessler's pre-2026 work on anion receptors and dimers, moving beyond simple halides to a physiologically relevant polyatomic oxoanion.

proposals.title: Anion-Binding Cavity Catalysis: A Carbaporphyrin Dimer Platform for Stabilizing Anionic Transition States in Organic Transformations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, supramolecular anion receptors, such as calix[4]pyrroles and expanded porphyrins developed by Sessler and others, are powerful for selective anion binding and transport. However, their direct application as catalysts, where the binding cavity stabilizes anionic transition states to accelerate organic reactions, remains a significant and underexplored frontier. This bridges supramolecular chemistry and catalysis, offering a new strategy for controlling reaction pathways and selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pre-organized, electron-deficient cavity of a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer, known to bind anions strongly, can be repurposed to stabilize anionic transition states, thereby catalyzing fundamental organic reactions like the Morita–Baylis–Hillman (MBH) reaction or nitro-Michael additions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer with a tunable, convergent anion-binding pocket. Its catalytic activity will be tested in model reactions known to proceed via anionic intermediates. Reaction kinetics, monitored by NMR and UV-vis spectroscopy, will be compared against controls (no catalyst, simple porphyrin). Anion binding studies (ITC, NMR titrations) with proposed anionic transition state analogues will establish structure-activity relationships. Computational modeling (DFT) will be used to visualize transition state stabilization within the cavity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of supramolecular catalysts derived from anion receptors, demonstrating a direct link between binding affinity and catalytic rate enhancement. It will provide a foundational design principle for 'cavity-induced transition state stabilization' applicable to other receptor scaffolds, advancing the field of supramolecular catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While anion receptors are well-known, their use as catalysts for general organic reactions by stabilizing anionic transition states is a non-obvious and largely unexplored concept. The specific application of a carbaporphyrin dimer, with its unique, tunable three-dimensional cavity, for this purpose is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Sterically Distorted Hemiporphycene Dimers
proposals.motivation: The anion-binding properties of porphyrinoids are profoundly influenced by macrocyclic conformation and preorganization. Pre-2026 work by Sessler and others has shown that non-planar, 'durch' structures in carbaporphyrin dimers create unique, magnified cavities for anion recognition. Hemiporphycene, a constitutional isomer of porphyrin, offers a distinct, less-explored scaffold where enforced non-planarity could unlock new host-guest chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing sterically demanding substituents (e.g., mesityl, 2,6-diisopropylphenyl) at key meso- and/or β-positions of the hemiporphycene framework will enforce a stable, non-planar 'saddle' or 'ruffled' conformation, and that dimerization of such distorted monomers will yield receptors with enhanced affinity and selectivity for specific anions, particularly fluoride.
proposals.method: We will synthesize hemiporphycene monomers bearing bulky aryl groups via established acid-catalyzed condensation routes, modifying procedures from Sessler's carbaporphyrin work. Conformational analysis will be performed using X-ray crystallography and DFT calculations. These monomers will be linked via flexible or rigid spacers (e.g., biphenyl, alkyne) to form dimers, creating preorganized clefts. Anion binding (F-, Cl-, H2PO4-, HSO4-) will be quantified using UV-vis and fluorescence titration methods in organic solvents, with results compared to planar controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship between steric distortion and anion affinity in the hemiporphycene class, a largely unexplored area. It will deliver the first family of non-planar hemiporphycene-based anion receptors, potentially identifying systems with utility as fluoride sensors or transport agents, expanding the toolbox of anion-responsive porphyrinoids.
proposals.novelty: While non-planar porphyrins and carbaporphyrins are known, the deliberate use of steric hindrance to distort the hemiporphycene core for anion recognition is unprecedented. The approach merges Sessler's expertise in anion receptors and distorted macrocycles to target a new, conformationally flexible platform.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Regioisomeric Hemiporphycenes: Probing the Role of Internal Nitrogen Arrangement
proposals.motivation: Hemiporphycene, a constitutional isomer of porphyrin with a contracted cavity and distinct photophysical properties, has shown promise as a scaffold for anion recognition. However, systematic studies on how the precise arrangement of its four internal nitrogen atoms influences its electronic structure, cavity shape, and anion affinity are lacking. Exploring these regioisomers will provide fundamental insights into structure-function relationships in contracted porphyrinoids, a key theme in the Sessler group's work on carbaporphyrins and anion receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that altering the sequence and relative positions of the pyrrolic and imino nitrogens within the hemiporphycene macrocycle will significantly modulate its dipole moment, cavity electron density, and selectivity for anions such as fluoride and chloride.
proposals.method: Target hemiporphycene regioisomers will be designed via retrosynthetic analysis, focusing on varying the connectivity of the bipyrrole and dipyrromethane-derived units. Synthesis will employ established acid-catalyzed condensation routes, similar to those used for carbaporphyrin dimers, with careful chromatographic separation of isomers. Characterization will involve NMR, X-ray crystallography, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. Anion binding studies will be conducted using UV-Vis and 1H NMR titrations in organic solvents (e.g., acetonitrile), quantifying association constants for halides and oxoanions, directly comparing the observed affinities to those of parent hemiporphycene and calixpyrrole models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first library of hemiporphycene regioisomers, mapping how nitrogen topology dictates macrocyclic properties. It will deliver new, tunable anion receptors and expand the conceptual framework for designing functional porphyrinoid isomers. The findings will directly contribute to the field of supramolecular chemistry focused on anion recognition and transport.
proposals.novelty: While porphyrin and carbaporphyrin isomers have been studied, a systematic investigation of hemiporphycene regioisomers is unprecedented. The approach moves beyond simple core modification to explore the fundamental consequences of permuting heteroatom positions within a single, contracted porphyrinoid class.

proposals.title: Hemiporphycene as a Tunable Heavy-Atom-Free Photosensitizer: Synthesis, Photophysical Characterization, and Anion-Binding Enhanced Singlet Oxygen Generation
proposals.motivation: The search for new, efficient, and tunable photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photocatalysis is a major pre-2026 challenge. Porphyrinoids like carbaporphyrins and expanded porphyrins show promise due to their modifiable electronic structures, but the specific photophysical properties of the non-aromatic, contracted isomer hemiporphycene remain largely unexplored for photosensitization. Its unique, distorted macrocyclic core could offer distinct excited-state dynamics compared to standard porphyrins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hemiporphycene, particularly when functionalized with anion-binding motifs like pyrrole or dipyrrolylquinoxaline units, will act as an effective heavy-atom-free photosensitizer, and that anion binding will modulate its photophysical properties to enhance intersystem crossing and singlet oxygen (1O2) quantum yield.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of hemiporphycene derivatives, including core structures and dimers linked through flexible or rigid spacers, inspired by Sessler's work on carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers. Key derivatives will be functionalized with known anion receptors (e.g., 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxaline or calix[4]pyrrole analogs). Photophysical characterization will involve UV-Vis-NIR absorption, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence, and phosphorescence spectroscopy. Singlet oxygen quantum yields (ΦΔ) will be measured using a chemical trap (1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran) and compared against standard sensitizers. The effect of anion binding (e.g., fluoride, chloride) on absorption, emission, and ΦΔ will be quantified.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive photophysical profile of hemiporphycene as a photosensitizer, determining its intrinsic triplet state yield and 1O2 generation efficiency. It will demonstrate a novel strategy for photosensitizer activation and tuning via supramolecular anion recognition, merging Sessler's expertise in anion receptors with porphyrinoid photochemistry. The results could identify a new class of stimuli-responsive, heavy-atom-free PDT agents.
proposals.novelty: While porphyrins and expanded porphyrins are well-studied as photosensitizers, the specific evaluation of the contracted, non-aromatic hemiporphycene isomer for this purpose is novel. The integration of anion-receptor appendages to directly control photosensitizing efficiency through host-guest chemistry, rather than covalent heavy-atom substitution, represents a non-obvious and innovative supramolecular approach.

proposals.title: Hemiporphycene as a Versatile Platform for Unusual Metal Coordination and Anion Sensing
proposals.motivation: Hemiporphycene, a porphyrinoid isomer with a contracted, non-aromatic cavity, has shown initial promise in forming stable complexes with metals like Pd(II) and Pt(II), as reported pre-2026. However, its full potential as a ligand, particularly for metals that prefer non-standard coordination geometries or for creating bifunctional metal-anion receptor systems, remains largely unexplored. Given the Sessler group's expertise in anion receptors and expanded porphyrinoids, investigating hemiporphycene's unique coordination chemistry could unlock new classes of functional materials and sensors.
proposals.hypothesis: The distorted, electron-rich cavity of hemiporphycene will selectively stabilize metal ions in unusual oxidation states or coordination geometries that are inaccessible to regular porphyrins, and these metallo-hemiporphycenes will exhibit enhanced anion-binding properties at peripheral sites.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize a series of hemiporphycene metal complexes (targeting Ag(I), Cu(I), Rh(I), and lanthanides(III)) using standard metallation procedures under inert atmosphere where necessary. Complexation will be confirmed via UV-Vis-NIR, NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. The anion affinity of these complexes will be evaluated via UV-Vis and NMR titration studies in organic solvents, focusing on fluoride, chloride, and phosphate anions, benchmarking against known anion receptors like calix[4]pyrroles. Electrochemical studies (cyclic voltammetry) will probe the electronic influence of the metal on the ligand.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the fundamental coordination chemistry of hemiporphycene, mapping its affinity for a broad range of metal ions. It will deliver the first examples of hemiporphycene-based bifunctional receptors that combine metal binding and anion recognition in a single scaffold, potentially leading to new sensing paradigms or catalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While porphyrin metal chemistry is mature, the systematic exploration of hemiporphycene's distinct cavity for stabilizing soft or low-valent metals is novel. Furthermore, integrating this with the group's anion receptor expertise to create 'through-space' communication between a metal center and an anion binding site is a non-obvious extension of prior work on Janus dimers and calixpyrroles.

proposals.title: Click-Functionalized Hemiporphycene Dimers as Tunable Anion Receptors and Transporters
proposals.motivation: Hemiporphycene, a porphyrin isomer, offers a distinct, non-symmetric core with untapped potential for supramolecular chemistry. Pre-2026 work by Sessler and others has demonstrated the power of expanded porphyrins and their dimers (e.g., Janus carbaporphyrins) in anion recognition and transport, but the peripheral functionalization of hemiporphycene remains underdeveloped. Efficiently installing diverse functional groups is crucial for tuning receptor properties, such as anion affinity and selectivity, for applications like sensing or transmembrane transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC) can be used to efficiently and modularly functionalize the hemiporphycene periphery, and that appending anion-binding motifs (e.g., pyrroles, amides) will yield dimeric structures with enhanced and tunable anion receptor properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a hemiporphycene core bearing multiple alkyne groups on its periphery. Using standard CuAAC conditions (CuSO4, sodium ascorbate), we will conjugate this core with azide-functionalized anion-binding units, such as pyrrole, 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxaline, or calix[4]pyrrole derivatives. The functionalized monomers will then be linked via 'through-space' or covalent bridges to form dimers, inspired by Sessler's 'durch' motif work. Anion binding will be characterized by UV-Vis/NMR titrations in organic solvents, and chloride/bicarbonate antiport activity will be assessed using established HPTS assay in lipid vesicle models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, modular platform for the post-synthetic modification of hemiporphycene, significantly expanding its chemical toolbox. It will produce a new family of anion receptors where the electronic and steric properties can be systematically varied via click chemistry, enabling structure-property relationship studies. The project may yield novel anion transporters with tailored activity, contributing to the development of synthetic ionophores.
proposals.novelty: While click chemistry has been applied to porphyrins, its use for the precise functionalization of the less-explored hemiporphycene isomer to create anion-responsive dimers is unprecedented. The approach merges Sessler's expertise in anion receptors with modern bioconjugation techniques to create structurally unique, multifunctional systems not accessible via traditional porphyrin synthesis.

proposals.title: Computational Elucidation of Hemiporphycene: Predicting Electronic Structure, Anion Binding, and Dimerization Potential
proposals.motivation: Hemiporphycene, a porphyrinoid isomer with a contracted, non-planar core, exhibits unique properties distinct from porphyrins and carbaporphyrins, but its electronic structure and reactivity remain underexplored. Given the Sessler group's expertise in carbaporphyrin dimers and anion-responsive macrocycles, a predictive computational study is crucial to guide targeted synthesis and application, particularly in anion sensing and supramolecular assembly, areas of intense pre-2026 interest.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that density functional theory (DFT) calculations will reveal that hemiporphycene possesses a distinct, tunable electronic structure with localized frontier molecular orbitals, predicting specific sites for electrophilic/nucleophilic attack and a propensity for selective anion binding and face-to-face dimerization driven by π-π and dipole-dipole interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ DFT calculations using the Gaussian 16 suite with the B3LYP functional and 6-311+G(d,p) basis set for geometry optimizations and electronic structure analysis of hemiporphycene and its protonated/deprotonated forms. Time-Dependent DFT (TD-DFT) will predict UV-Vis spectra for comparison with known data. Anion binding affinities (e.g., for F-, Cl-) will be calculated via Gibbs free energy changes, and potential dimerization modes will be explored using dispersion-corrected functionals (e.g., ωB97XD) to model intermolecular interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive computational map of hemiporphycene's electronic and steric landscape, predicting its reactivity and supramolecular behavior. It will directly inform the design of new hemiporphycene-based anion receptors and dimers, bridging the gap between known carbaporphyrin chemistry and this less-studied isomer, and establish a computational framework for related porphyrinoids.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is routinely applied to porphyrins, its targeted use to deconvolute the properties of hemiporphycene—specifically its anion binding potential and dimerization drivers—in direct context of Sessler's work on carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers and anion receptors is novel and non-obvious, offering a predictive roadmap rather than a post-synthetic analysis.

proposals.title: Heterometallic Janus Carbaporphyrin Dimers: Synthesis, Characterization, and Anion-Binding Modulation via Metal Pairing
proposals.motivation: The Sessler group's pioneering synthesis of a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer, featuring a Cu(III) center and a BODIPY unit, established a unique platform for creating heterometallic complexes with potentially orthogonal electronic and binding properties. Prior to 2026, the exploration of such dimers with other metal pairs remains unexplored, limiting our understanding of how metal identity influences the structure, spectroscopy, and anion-receptor function of these sophisticated macrocyclic assemblies. This gap presents a significant opportunity to expand the chemistry of carbaporphyrin dimers and design new multifunctional systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coordinating distinct pairs of redox-active, Lewis acidic, or luminescent metals (e.g., Pd(II)/Zn(II), Ni(II)/Al(III), or Pt(II)/Re(I)) into the two distinct coordination pockets of the Janus dimer scaffold, we can systematically tune the electronic structure, photophysical properties, and anion-binding affinity of the resulting complexes in a predictable and magnified manner compared to their monometallic counterparts.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the parent Janus carbaporphyrin dimer ligand based on established pre-2026 procedures. Sequential or one-pot metalation reactions will be employed using metal salts (e.g., Pd(OAc)2, Zn(OAc)2, Ni(acac)2, AlCl3, PtCl2, Re(CO)5Cl) under controlled conditions to achieve selective incorporation. Complexes will be characterized by NMR, HR-MS, UV-Vis-NIR absorption, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Anion-binding studies, focusing on fluoride and chloride as per group expertise, will be conducted using UV-Vis and fluorescence titration methods in organic solvents to quantify binding constants and probe receptor behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first library of well-defined heterometallic Janus carbaporphyrin dimers, establishing structure-property relationships between metal pair identity and functional output. It will contribute fundamental knowledge on cooperative effects in dimeric porphyrinoid systems and potentially yield new anion sensors or catalysts with enhanced or novel selectivities derived from the heterometallic core.
proposals.novelty: While heterometallic porphyrin dimers exist, applying this concept to the unique, non-symmetric Janus carbaporphyrin scaffold—where the two metal centers are intrinsically different due to the ligand framework—is unprecedented. The deliberate pairing of metals beyond the initial Cu(III)/BODIPY system to probe and magnify anion-receptor interactions represents a novel and logical extension of the group's recent work.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimers with Tunable Electronic Substituents
proposals.motivation: The Sessler group's pioneering work on Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers, such as the bischinone-substituted system, has demonstrated unique through-space electronic communication and anion-binding cooperativity. However, the strong electron-accepting nature of quinone substituents dominates the electronic structure, masking the intrinsic properties of the dimeric carbaporphyrin scaffold. Systematically relaxing this substitution is crucial to decouple the core dimer's behavior from the influence of a strong acceptor, enabling a fundamental study of structure-property relationships in these novel architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the bischinone units in carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers with electronically neutral or donating substituents will significantly modulate the system's redox potential, frontier orbital energies, and anion-binding affinity/selectivity, revealing the scaffold's inherent electronic communication and cooperative binding without the masking effect of a strong acceptor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers via established acid-catalyzed condensation routes, varying the bridging/terminal substituents. Targets will include alkyl/aryl (electron-neutral), dialkylamino (electron-donating), and unsubstituted (proton) variants. The compounds will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, UV-Vis-NIR, and cyclic voltammetry. Anion binding studies will be conducted using UV-Vis and fluorescence titration methods in organic solvents (e.g., dichloromethane, acetonitrile) with tetrabutylammonium salts of fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate, quantifying binding constants and stoichiometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property map for carbaporphyrin dimers, defining how electronic modulation tunes their optical, redox, and host-guest properties. It will reveal the intrinsic anion-binding cooperativity of the dimeric core and may lead to new receptors with tailored selectivities, moving beyond the quinone-dominated paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While carbaporphyrin dimers with quinone acceptors are known, the systematic investigation of their properties with neutral or donating groups is unexplored. This approach deliberately removes the dominant electronic feature to probe the fundamental behavior of the dimeric scaffold, a non-obvious but critical step for rational design.

proposals.title: Solubility-Engineered Carbaporphyrinoids: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Highly Soluble, Sterically Encumbered Macrocycles
proposals.motivation: Large, π-conjugated porphyrinoids often suffer from poor solubility, hindering their synthesis, characterization, and application in solution-phase processes like sensing or transport. Pre-2026 work, including Sessler's carbaporphyrin dimers and anion receptors, demonstrates the functional potential of expanded macrocycles, but their utility is frequently limited by aggregation and precipitation. Prioritizing solubility from the outset is crucial for translating complex molecular designs into practical, processable materials.
proposals.hypothesis: By intentionally incorporating bulky, solubilizing substituents (e.g., tert-butyl, triethylene glycol chains, or perfluoroalkyl groups) at key positions on the pyrrolic and aryl components, we can synthesize large, functional carbaporphyrinoid scaffolds that retain excellent solubility in common organic solvents without requiring extreme structural distortion of the core π-system.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of carbaporphyrin monomers and dimers based on known scaffolds (e.g., Janus-type structures) but with a systematic variation of solubilizing groups. The synthetic approach will leverage Sessler's expertise in porphyrinoid chemistry, using Adler-Longo or Lindsey-type condensations modified with pre-functionalized precursors. Key steps will include introducing 2,6-di-tert-butylphenyl or polyether chains at meso- or β-positions. Solubility will be quantitatively assessed in CH2Cl2, THF, and toluene. Anion-binding studies (e.g., with fluoride and chloride) via UV-vis and 1H NMR titrations will evaluate if the enhanced solubility compromises the receptor function.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design paradigm for creating solution-processable, large porphyrinoids, directly addressing a major bottleneck in the field. It will yield new, well-characterized macrocycles whose solubility profiles are documented, enabling their broader use in supramolecular chemistry. The project will also reveal how steric encumbrance from solubilizing groups modulates anion-binding affinity and selectivity.
proposals.novelty: While solubilizing groups are known, their use as the primary design driver for carbaporphyrinoids, superseding the goal of maximizing conjugation, is a distinct and pragmatic strategy. Combining Sessler's unique carbaporphyrin and anion receptor expertise to create soluble, yet functionally active, large macrocycles is an unexplored pathway.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Characterization of the Free-Base Turcasarin Macrocycle: Probing the Electronic Structure of a Carbaporphyrinoid Dimer
proposals.motivation: The Sessler group's recent synthesis of the 'Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer,' Turcasarin, represents a significant advance in expanded porphyrin chemistry, creating a unique platform with two distinct, non-equivalent cavities. However, its initial characterization focused on the protonated form. The electronic structure and anion-binding properties of its free base, which lacks internal protons, remain unexplored. Understanding this state is crucial for elucidating the fundamental photophysical and host-guest properties intrinsic to this novel dimeric architecture, independent of protonation effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the free-base Turcasarin will exhibit a distinct, red-shifted absorption profile and altered frontier molecular orbital energies compared to its protonated form, and that its two cavities will display differentiated, cooperative anion-binding behavior due to their inherent electronic asymmetry.
proposals.method: The free-base Turcasarin will be prepared via careful deprotonation of the known protonated macrocycle using mild, non-nucleophilic bases (e.g., DBU) in rigorously dried, degassed solvents, followed by chromatographic purification. Comprehensive characterization will involve high-resolution mass spectrometry, multinuclear NMR (1H, 13C, 19F), and UV-Vis-NIR absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Electronic structure will be probed via cyclic voltammetry and supported by DFT calculations (e.g., B3LYP/6-31G(d)) to map HOMO-LUMO gaps and molecular electrostatic potentials. Initial anion-binding studies will be conducted using UV-Vis and fluorescence titrations with tetrabutylammonium salts of fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete spectroscopic and electrochemical dataset for the free-base form of a Janus carbaporphyrin dimer, establishing a foundational understanding of its core electronic properties. It will reveal how protonation state modulates the optical and redox behavior of complex carbaporphyrinoid systems and will assess the innate anion affinity of this unique dimeric receptor scaffold.
proposals.novelty: While anion receptors based on dipyrrolylquinoxalines and calix[4]pyrroles are well-established in the Sessler portfolio, studying the *free-base* of a pre-formed, asymmetric carbaporphyrin dimer is unprecedented. This moves beyond simple anion sensing to a detailed dissection of how dimerization and cavity differentiation within a single macrocycle influence electronic structure without the confounding variable of N-H protons.

proposals.title: Enantioselective Separation and Chiroptical Analysis of a Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimer: Towards Chiral Anion Recognition
proposals.motivation: The recent synthesis of a twisted, non-planar Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer introduces inherent chirality into the porphyrinoid framework, a feature rarely explored in carbaporphyrin chemistry. Pre-2026, the Sessler group has pioneered anion receptors based on pyrrolic and expanded porphyrin systems, but the potential for chiral discrimination in anion binding using such distorted, racemic architectures remains untested. Resolving the enantiomers of this unique dimer is a critical first step to unlock applications in chiral sensing and asymmetric catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The twisted enantiomers of the Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer can be separated and will exhibit distinct, strong chiroptical signals (electronic circular dichroism, ECD) and enantioselective binding affinities for chiral anions or differentially for prochiral anions in a chiral environment.
proposals.method: We will first employ chiral high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) using amylose- or cellulose-based stationary phases to resolve the racemic mixture of the pseudo-dimer. The isolated enantiomers will be characterized by ECD spectroscopy and optical rotation. Their absolute configuration will be assigned via time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations of ECD spectra. Anion binding studies will then be conducted using UV-vis-NIR and fluorescence titrations with a series of anions (e.g., fluoride, chloride, carboxylates), including chiral anions like L- and D-tartrate, to probe enantioselective interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first chiroptical signature and absolute configuration assignment for a chiral carbaporphyrin dimer, establishing a structure-property relationship for its twisted π-system. It will evaluate the fundamental potential of distorted porphyrinoids for chiral anion recognition, potentially leading to a new class of enantioselective sensors or extractants, thereby bridging Sessler's expertise in anion receptors and non-planar porphyrinoids.
proposals.novelty: While the synthesis of the Janus dimer is known, its chirality has been entirely unexplored. This proposal moves beyond structural characterization to actively resolve, analyze, and functionally test the enantiomers, combining chiral separation, computational assignment, and enantioselective anion binding studies—a novel integration for carbaporphyrin systems.

proposals.title: Metal Ion Coordination Chemistry of a Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimer: Probing the Limits of Expanded and Non-Innocent Macrocyclic Ligands
proposals.motivation: The Sessler group's recent synthesis of a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer represents a significant expansion of the porphyrinoid ligand family, creating a large, conformationally flexible macrocycle with multiple potential donor sets. Pre-2026, the metal coordination chemistry of such complex, non-innocent carbaporphyrin dimers remains largely unexplored. Investigating this is crucial to determine if these novel ligands can stabilize unusual metal oxidation states, coordination geometries, or exhibit cooperative binding effects, potentially unlocking new reactivity paradigms distinct from classic porphyrins.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique, expanded cavity and mixed donor-atom environment (N, C) of the Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer will enable it to act as a versatile, non-innocent ligand, forming stable complexes with a range of metal ions (e.g., transition metals, lanthanides) and potentially inducing novel electronic structures or metal-centered reactivity not accessible with standard porphyrins.
proposals.method: The pseudo-dimer will be treated with metal salts (e.g., Ni(II), Cu(II), Pd(II), Ag(I), Zn(II), Lu(III)) under varied conditions (solvent, temperature, base). Complex formation will be monitored by UV-vis-NIR and fluorescence spectroscopy, tracking characteristic porphyrinoid band shifts. Isolated complexes will be fully characterized by 1H/19F NMR, high-resolution mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Electrochemical studies (cyclic voltammetry) will assess the ligand's non-innocence and the complexes' redox properties. Anion binding studies (via UV-vis/fluorescence titration with TBAF, TBAHPO4) will be conducted on metallated forms to probe any metal-anion cooperative allosteric effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the foundational coordination chemistry of a new class of expanded carbaporphyrin dimers, mapping their ligand field properties and chelating ability. It will reveal structure-property relationships between macrocyclic distortion and metal ion selectivity/redox tuning. The project may yield the first examples of metal complexes where the carbaporphyrin dimer acts as a redox-active or anion-responsive platform, bridging Sessler's expertise in anion receptors and porphyrinoid synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While metallation of monomeric carbaporphyrins is known, the coordination behavior of a dimeric, Janus-faced macrocycle of this size and complexity is unprecedented. The explicit investigation of whether metal binding modulates the inherent anion receptor properties (e.g., fluoride binding) of the pyrrolic subunits creates a novel, synergistic research direction at the intersection of coordination chemistry and supramolecular anion recognition.

proposals.title: A Quinone-Primed, Porphyrin-Acceptor Supramolecular Dyad for Inverted Photoinduced Electron Transfer
proposals.motivation: In artificial photosynthetic models and organic photovoltaics, porphyrins are almost universally employed as the primary light-harvesting and electron-donating components, with quinones acting as terminal electron acceptors. This conventional donor-acceptor paradigm limits exploration of alternative charge-separation pathways and the photophysical properties of quinones, which are strong UV absorbers but underutilized as photoactive centers. Investigating a role reversal could unveil new design principles for charge separation and energy conversion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically linking a strongly electron-deficient, photoexcitable quinone unit to an electron-rich porphyrin macrocycle, we can engineer a supramolecular system where photoexcitation of the quinone leads to efficient electron transfer to the porphyrin, creating a long-lived charge-separated state with an inverted polarity relative to conventional models.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a covalently linked dyad using a 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxaline-derived scaffold, analogous to Sessler's anion receptor work, to tether a naphthoquinone or anthraquinone unit to a carbaporphyrin or standard porphyrin. The quinone will be functionalized to enhance visible light absorption. Photophysical characterization will involve steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, nanosecond transient absorption spectroscopy, and electrochemical studies to quantify excited-state energies, electron transfer rates, and charge-separated state lifetimes. Computational modeling (TD-DFT) will guide design and interpret results.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for inverted donor-acceptor photo-dynamics in porphyrinoid-quinone systems, expanding the toolkit for molecular electronics. It will provide fundamental insights into quinone photophysics within a constrained dyad and could lead to novel redox-active materials with unique light-driven properties.
proposals.novelty: While porphyrin-quinone dyads are well-studied, explicitly designing the quinone as the primary photoabsorber and electron donor to a porphyrin acceptor is a fundamentally inverted and unexplored approach, moving beyond the established paradigm.

proposals.title: Mapping Through-Space Superexchange Pathways in Carbaporphyrin-Quinone Architectures: A Geometric Dependence Study
proposals.motivation: Understanding and controlling electron transfer (ET) pathways is fundamental to bioenergetics and molecular electronics. Superexchange, a critical mechanism for long-range ET, is highly sensitive to the spatial arrangement and electronic coupling of donor-bridge-acceptor systems. Pre-2026 work, including Sessler's on carbaporphyrin dimers and quinoxaline-based anion receptors, provides a unique toolkit to construct structurally diverse, non-planar scaffolds where geometry can be precisely varied to probe this sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficiency of superexchange-mediated electron transfer between a porphyrin donor and a quinone acceptor will exhibit a non-linear, geometry-dependent relationship, with through-space pathways across rigid, cofacially arranged aromatic surfaces providing superior coupling compared to more flexible or edge-on arrangements at equivalent distances.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of covalently linked carbaporphyrin-quinone dyads using Sessler's carbaporphyrin and dipyrrolylquinoxaline chemistry as synthetic platforms. The spatial geometry (cofacial, orthogonal, staggered) and center-to-center distance will be systematically controlled using rigid, shape-persistent linkers like phenylacetylene spacers or through the design of 'Janus'-type dimeric frameworks. ET rates will be measured via femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy following photoexcitation, and electronic coupling matrix elements (V) will be extracted using Marcus theory analysis. DFT and TD-DFT calculations (B3LYP/6-31G*) will model frontier orbitals and coupling pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, experimental map of superexchange efficiency as a function of 3D molecular geometry, moving beyond simple distance dependence. It will establish design principles for next-generation molecular wires and artificial photosynthetic centers by identifying optimal through-space coupling motifs. The study will also demonstrate the utility of carbaporphyrins as versatile, tunable platforms for fundamental ET studies.
proposals.novelty: While distance dependence of ET is well-studied, systematic investigation of the geometric dependence of through-space superexchange in well-defined, rigid, non-porphyrinoid macrocycle-quinone systems is unexplored. Leveraging carbaporphyrin's distinct non-planarity and anion-binding quinoxaline units to control and probe geometry represents a novel chemical approach to this physical problem.

proposals.title: Mapping Energy Transfer Pathways and Electronic Coherence in a Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Trimer via Ultrafast Two-Dimensional Electronic Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Jonathan Sessler's pioneering work on carbaporphyrin dimers and expanded porphyrins has created complex, non-planar architectures with unique electronic properties and anion-binding capabilities. Understanding the ultrafast energy flow and electronic couplings in these multi-chromophore systems is crucial for designing advanced molecular devices for light harvesting, sensing, and information processing, but such dynamics remain largely unmapped due to their structural complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ultrafast 2D electronic spectroscopy will reveal directional, coherent energy transfer pathways and specific electronic couplings between the distinct macrocyclic units in the carbaporphyrin trimer, dynamics that are obscured in conventional time-resolved measurements.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the target carbaporphyrin trimer, building on Sessler's established routes for Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers. Ultrafast 2D electronic spectra will be acquired using a phase-stable, passively stabilized interferometer setup with a broadband NOPA laser source (~500-750 nm). We will employ the pump-probe geometry with heterodyne detection, collecting spectra at a series of population times (T) from 0 fs to several picoseconds. Data analysis will utilize singular value decomposition (SVD) and global fitting to extract decay-associated spectra, followed by modeling with the secular generalized Förster theory to quantify coupling strengths and energy transfer rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-resolved map of electronic communication in a carbaporphyrin oligomer, quantifying coupling strengths and energy transfer efficiencies. It will establish a foundational photophysical understanding for designing next-generation porphyrinoid-based materials with tailored excited-state properties for anion-responsive optoelectronics or artificial photosynthetic systems.
proposals.novelty: While 2D spectroscopy has been applied to natural light-harvesting complexes and standard porphyrin arrays, its application to structurally distorted, anion-responsive carbaporphyrin oligomers—where electronic communication is mediated through non-standard linkages and is potentially modulated by anion binding—is unprecedented and leverages Sessler's unique synthetic expertise.

proposals.title: A Minimalist Porphyrin-Quinone Dyad: Synthesis, Anion-Binding Modulation, and Ground-State Electronic Communication
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work from the Sessler group and others has established that complex, multi-chromophore architectures like carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers exhibit fascinating emergent properties, including through-space electronic communication and anion-responsive behavior. However, the fundamental pairwise interaction between a single porphyrinoid donor and a quinone acceptor, especially under the influence of anionic guests, remains obscured in these larger systems. Isolating this minimal unit is critical for a foundational understanding of electron and energy transfer pathways relevant to biomimetics and molecular electronics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synthetically accessible, covalently linked porphyrin-quinone dyad will exhibit measurable ground-state electronic interaction, and that this interaction can be predictably modulated by the binding of specific anions (e.g., fluoride) to a proximal, integrated anion receptor site.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a target dyad by linking a mono-functionalized porphyrin (e.g., tetraphenylporphyrin or a carbaporphyrin analog) to a quinone unit via a conformationally constrained, non-conjugated bridge. An anion-binding motif, such as a 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxaline or a calix[4]pyrrole-derived unit, will be integrated into the bridge or porphyrin periphery. The dyad will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Electronic communication will be probed via UV-Vis-NIR absorption, cyclic voltammetry, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Anion modulation experiments will track spectroscopic and electrochemical shifts upon titration with tetrabutylammonium salts (F-, Cl-, H2PO4-).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark dataset on the intrinsic electronic coupling in a donor-acceptor pair decoupled from multi-chromophore effects. It will establish clear structure-property relationships for anion-gated electronic communication, a key mechanism for developing chemosensors and molecular switches. The findings will serve as a critical reference for interpreting data from more complex 'durch' and 'magnified' dimeric systems.
proposals.novelty: While porphyrin-quinone dyads are known, none have been designed with integrated, pre-2026 Sessler-group anion receptors specifically to probe anion-gated ground-state electronic effects. This approach moves beyond studying excited-state processes or simple binding, focusing on anion as a direct modulator of the dyad's electronic structure.

proposals.title: Kinetic and Mechanistic Analysis of Charge Recombination in Quinone-Appended Carbaporphyrin Dimers
proposals.motivation: Inspired by natural photosynthesis, extensive pre-2026 research has focused on creating artificial donor-acceptor systems, such as porphyrin-quinone dyads, to achieve long-lived charge separation for solar energy conversion. However, the 'inverse problem'—the detailed pathway and kinetics of electron return (charge recombination)—is less understood but critically determines the ultimate efficiency of energy storage. A systematic study of this recombination process in novel, structurally defined carbaporphyrin scaffolds is needed to establish design rules for retarding back-electron transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unique electronic structure and conformational flexibility of a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer, when linked to a quinone acceptor, will create multiple, tunable recombination pathways whose kinetics can be controlled by anion binding and structural preorganization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of quinone-appended Janus carbaporphyrin dimers, leveraging Sessler's expertise in carbaporphyrin and dimer synthesis. Photoinduced electron transfer will be initiated using a nanosecond laser flash photolysis setup. The charge recombination kinetics will be measured via time-resolved absorption spectroscopy in solvents of varying polarity and in the presence of anions like fluoride and chloride, utilizing the group's established anion receptor motifs (e.g., calix[4]pyrrole derivatives) to modulate the system's redox potential and geometry. Global target analysis of the multi-wavelength transient data will be used to resolve distinct kinetic components and spectral intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental mechanistic map of charge recombination in a non-porphyrinoid framework, directly linking structural perturbation via anion binding to recombination rates. It will establish carbaporphyrin dimers as a versatile platform for studying and controlling electron transfer dynamics, offering new strategies for the design of molecular electronics and artificial photosynthetic systems with suppressed energy-wasting back-reactions.
proposals.novelty: While porphyrin-quinone dyads are well-known, applying the 'inverse problem' lens to structurally innovative, anion-responsive carbaporphyrin dimers is novel. The approach uniquely combines conformational control (from the dimer) with dynamic electronic modulation (via anion receptors) to dissect recombination pathways in a way not possible with classical porphyrins.

proposals.title: Enforcing Planarity in Sapphyrins: Synthesis of Strapped and Fused Derivatives for Anion Binding and Transport Studies
proposals.motivation: Sapphyrin, a core expanded porphyrin, is a potent anion receptor but typically adopts a non-planar, 'wavy' conformation in solution and the solid state. This inherent flexibility complicates the analysis of its anion-binding properties and limits the development of materials with predictable electronic structures derived from a rigid, conjugated macrocycle. Forcing a planar conformation would create a new class of rigid, large-area π-systems with potentially enhanced anion affinity and unique photophysical properties, directly extending the Sessler group's expertise in anion-responsive macrocycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategic substitution and the introduction of covalent straps or ring fusion will enforce a planar conformation in sapphyrin, leading to significantly altered anion binding constants, selectivity profiles (particularly for fluoride and chloride), and enhanced transmembrane anion transport efficacy compared to the flexible parent macrocycle.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of sapphyrin derivatives designed to enforce planarity: 1) β-pyrrole substitution with bulky tert-butyl groups to induce steric flattening, 2) covalent bridging ('strapping') of the macrocycle across the meso-positions using rigid linkers like diphenylethyne, and 3) ring fusion via oxidative cyclization of adjacent pyrroles. Complexation with anions (F-, Cl-, H2PO4-) will be studied using UV-Vis-NIR and 1H/19F NMR titrations in dichloromethane and DMSO to determine binding constants. Planarity will be confirmed by X-ray crystallography. Chloride/bicarbonate antiport activity will be assessed using HPTS assay in POPC vesicles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationships linking enforced sapphyrin planarity to anion recognition and transport. It will deliver novel, rigid expanded porphyrin architectures with potential applications in sensing and as functional components in supramolecular materials. The project will fundamentally challenge the understanding of sapphyrin conformational dynamics and its role in function.
proposals.novelty: While sapphyrin chemistry is established, systematically enforcing and studying its planar conformation via strain is unexplored. This approach merges the group's deep knowledge of anion receptors with a conformational engineering strategy typically applied to porphyrins, but not yet to their expanded analogues like sapphyrin.

proposals.title: Electronic and Anion-Binding Modulation in Carbaporphyrin Metal Complexes via Axial Ligand Substitution: From Carbonyls to Phosphines, Nitriles, and N-Heterocyclic Carbenes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Sessler and others has established carbaporphyrins and their metal complexes as unique, non-innocent platforms for anion recognition and sensing, with properties highly sensitive to the core structure and metal identity. The axial carbonyl ligands ubiquitous in many synthetic metalloporphyrinoids are strong π-acceptors that significantly perturb the metal's electronic structure, which in turn dictates the macrocycle's anion affinity and optical response. Systematically replacing these carbonyls with donors of differing σ-donor/π-acceptor character represents a direct, underexplored route to fine-tune these critical properties for tailored receptor design.
proposals.hypothesis: Substituting the axial carbonyl ligands on metal-carbaporphyrin complexes with stronger σ-donors (e.g., N-heterocyclic carbenes) or neutral π-acceptors (e.g., nitriles) will dramatically alter the metal's electron density and oxidation state, thereby modulating the macrocycle's Lewis acidity, anion-binding affinity, and resulting spectroscopic (UV-Vis/NMR) shifts upon anion coordination.
proposals.method: We will synthesize known metal-carbaporphyrin carbonyl complexes (e.g., Rh(I), Ir(I)) as starting materials. These will undergo ligand substitution reactions under controlled atmospheres (N2/Ar) with phosphines (PPh3, PCy3), nitriles (CH3CN, PhCN), and N-heterocyclic carbenes (e.g., IMeS). Products will be characterized by NMR, HR-MS, and X-ray crystallography. Anion binding studies will be conducted via UV-Vis and 1H/19F NMR titrations in dichloromethane, quantifying association constants (Ka) for fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate, and comparing them directly to the carbonyl parent complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear structure-activity relationship between axial ligand identity and anion-binding efficacy in carbaporphyrin systems, providing a new synthetic handle for optimizing receptor performance. It will deliver a novel family of tunable anion sensors and expand the fundamental understanding of how peripheral ligand modifications electronically communicate with the carbaporphyrin core to influence supramolecular function.
proposals.novelty: While axial ligand substitution is common in porphyrins, its application to modulate the anion-recognition properties of anti-aromatic carbaporphyrin metal complexes is unprecedented. The focus on replacing the intrinsically π-acidic carbonyl with strong σ-donors like NHCs to potentially enhance anion binding through increased metal Lewis acidity is a novel strategy distinct from classic porphyrinoid modifications.

proposals.title: Sapphyrin as a Redox-Active, Non-Innocent Ligand for Anion-Coupled Electron Transfer Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins like sapphyrin are established anion receptors due to their large, electron-rich cavities. However, their rich redox chemistry, stemming from their conjugated, aromatic 22-π-electron system, remains underexploited in catalysis. Integrating anion recognition with redox activity could enable new catalytic cycles for challenging transformations, such as the functionalization of inert substrates, where simultaneous substrate binding and multi-electron transfer are required.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metal complexes of sapphyrin will act as efficient catalysts for anion-coupled electron transfer (ACET) reactions, where the sapphyrin macrocycle itself serves as a non-innocent, redox-active ligand, storing and delivering electrons while the cavity templates and activates an anionic substrate.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of first-row transition metal (e.g., Co, Ni, Cu) complexes of sapphyrin, varying the metal center and peripheral substituents. Their anion-binding affinities (for fluoride, chloride, phosphate) will be quantified via UV-Vis and NMR titrations in non-aqueous media. The redox properties of the free sapphyrin and its metal complexes will be characterized by cyclic voltammetry and spectroelectrochemistry. These complexes will then be tested as catalysts in model ACET reactions, such as the dehalogenation of aryl halides or the reduction of O₂, monitoring catalysis via spectroscopic and electrochemical methods to establish the role of the sapphyrin π-system in the redox cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for using expanded porphyrins as active redox components in catalysis, moving beyond their traditional role as passive receptors or photosensitizers. It will provide fundamental insights into anion-coupled electron transfer processes and deliver a new class of tunable, multi-electron transfer catalysts with built-in substrate recognition sites.
proposals.novelty: While Sessler's prior work used sapphyrin for anion sensing and transport, this proposal explicitly treats the macrocycle as a non-innocent, redox-adaptable platform for catalysis, a significant conceptual leap from its established applications. The deliberate fusion of anion receptor chemistry with the redox activity of an expanded porphyrin ligand in a catalytic cycle is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Substrate Scope Exploration of a Bimetallic Sapphyrin Catalyst for the Hydrogenation of Diverse Alkenes
proposals.motivation: The development of new hydrogenation catalysts, particularly those based on expanded porphyrinoids like sapphyrins, is a key challenge in synthetic chemistry. While Sessler's group has demonstrated the synthesis of bimetallic sapphyrin dimers and their preliminary activity in cyclohexene hydrogenation, the full catalytic potential and substrate generality of these unique, multimetallic macrocycles remain unexplored. Establishing a broad substrate scope is critical to validate their utility and guide future catalyst design for selective transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: The bimetallic sapphyrin catalyst, by virtue of its dual metal centers and rigid, pre-organized macrocyclic framework, will exhibit significant catalytic activity for the hydrogenation of a range of alkenes beyond cyclohexene, with selectivity influenced by alkene sterics and electronics.
proposals.method: The study will employ the previously reported bimetallic sapphyrin dimer as the catalyst. A systematic series of alkenes will be tested under standardized hydrogenation conditions (e.g., 1-10 bar H2, 25-80°C, in a suitable solvent like toluene or THF). Substrates will include linear and branched terminal alkenes (e.g., 1-octene, styrene), internal alkenes (e.g., trans-2-octene, cyclooctene), and functionalized alkenes (e.g., methyl acrylate, allyl alcohol). Reaction progress will be monitored by GC-MS or NMR spectroscopy, with conversion and turnover numbers calculated to quantify activity and compare substrate reactivity trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evaluation of the catalytic scope of a bimetallic sapphyrin system, directly linking macrocyclic structure to function. It will identify promising substrate classes for this catalyst type and reveal structure-activity relationships that inform the design of next-generation expanded porphyrin catalysts for hydrogenation and related reductions.
proposals.novelty: While sapphyrins are known as anion receptors, their application as multimetallic catalysts for organic transformations is nascent. Extending the initial cyclohexene result to a broad substrate scope is a non-obvious and necessary step to transition this system from a curiosity to a potentially general catalytic platform.

proposals.title: Synthesis, Characterization, and Anion-Binding Properties of Mononuclear Iridium(III) Carbonyl Complexes with Sapphyrin Ligands
proposals.motivation: Sapphyrin, a pentapyrrolic expanded porphyrin, is a pre-2026 established anion receptor due to its large, tunable cavity and rich coordination chemistry. The integration of a heavy transition metal like iridium, known for its photophysical and catalytic properties, into a sapphyrin scaffold is unexplored. This combination could yield novel bifunctional platforms that merge anion recognition with metal-centered reactivity or luminescence, advancing the design of supramolecular sensors and catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sapphyrin can act as a macrocyclic, monoanionic ligand for Ir(I) or Ir(III) centers, forming stable, mononuclear carbonyl complexes where the metal's electronic properties are modulated by the expanded porphyrin core, and that the resulting complex will retain or exhibit enhanced anion-binding affinity compared to free sapphyrin.
proposals.method: The project will first synthesize sapphyrin free base and its deprotonated forms. These will be reacted with iridium carbonyl precursors, such as [Ir(CO)₂Cl]₂, under inert atmosphere to target mononuclear complexes. Products will be purified by column chromatography and characterized by ¹H/¹⁹F NMR, high-resolution mass spectrometry, IR spectroscopy (to confirm CO stretches), and X-ray crystallography. Anion-binding studies will be conducted via UV-Vis and fluorescence titration in dichloromethane, focusing on fluoride and chloride, using established pre-2026 protocols from the Sessler group.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first well-characterized family of iridium-sapphyrin hybrids, establishing a new class of organometallic-expanded porphyrin materials. It will provide fundamental insights into the coordination chemistry of sapphyrin with late transition metals and reveal how metalation influences the macrocycle's anion receptor properties, potentially leading to anion-responsive phosphorescent sensors.
proposals.novelty: While iridium porphyrin complexes are known, their synthesis with the larger, more flexible, and anion-binding sapphyrin macrocycle is unprecedented. The proposed complexes uniquely position an iridium carbonyl moiety within a pre-organized anion-binding site, a non-obvious strategy to create integrated recognition-and-reporting systems.

proposals.title: Sapphyrin-Metal Complexes as Versatile Catalysts: Expanding Beyond C–H Activation to Hydroformylation and Transfer Hydrogenation
