proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins like sapphyrins, pioneered by the Sessler group, offer unique, tunable coordination environments distinct from standard porphyrins, enabling unusual metal oxidation states and reactivity. While recent pre-2026 work has demonstrated their efficacy in challenging C–H activation, their potential in other industrially relevant catalytic transformations remains largely unexplored. Establishing sapphyrin-metal complexes as a general platform for catalysis would significantly broaden the impact of core-modified porphyrinoid chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: The flexible, electron-rich cavity and anion-binding capabilities of sapphyrins, when complexed with late transition metals like Rh or Ir, will create highly active and selective catalysts for hydroformylation and transfer hydrogenation by stabilizing key intermediates and modulating substrate approach.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of sapphyrin ligands with varied peripheral substituents and metalate them with Rh(I), Ir(I), and Ru(II) precursors. These complexes will be screened as catalysts for the hydroformylation of styrene and 1-octene under mild conditions (e.g., 80°C, 20 bar syngas), analyzing regioselectivity (n/iso ratio) by GC-MS. Parallelly, they will be tested in the transfer hydrogenation of acetophenone using i-PrOH/KOH or formic acid/triethylamine, monitoring conversion by NMR. Catalytic performance will be correlated with structural data (X-ray, NMR) and anion-binding studies (e.g., with phosphate or formate) to elucidate mechanistic roles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic evaluation of sapphyrin-metal complexes in two major catalytic classes, potentially revealing superior activity or unique selectivity profiles. It will provide fundamental structure-activity relationships for metallosapphyrin catalysts, linking anion receptor properties to catalytic function, and open a new design axis for porphyrinoid-based catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While sapphyrins are known anion receptors and have been used in C–H activation, their application to hydroformylation or transfer hydrogenation is unprecedented. The novelty lies in leveraging their inherent anion-binding capacity, a pre-2026 hallmark of Sessler's work, to potentially stabilize anionic transition states or substrates in these catalytic cycles.

proposals.title: Computational Elucidation of Bimetallic Cooperative Effects in a Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimer: A DFT Study on Structure, Anion Binding, and Catalytic Potential
proposals.motivation: The Sessler group's synthesis of a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer presents a unique, structurally defined platform for bimetallic coordination, a key motif in bioinorganic catalysis and anion recognition. Pre-2026, the experimental characterization of such complexes provides limited insight into the electronic structure, metal-metal communication, and the precise mechanisms of cooperative substrate activation. A detailed computational study is crucial to decode these interactions and guide the rational design of new catalysts and receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DFT modeling will reveal significant electronic coupling and cooperative binding effects between two metal centers (e.g., Pd(II), Pt(II), or Cu(II)) embedded within the Janus dimer framework, leading to altered redox properties, enhanced anion affinity, and a lowered energetic barrier for the activation of small molecules (e.g., O2 or N2) compared to monometallic analogs.
proposals.method: We will employ Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the Gaussian 16 suite. Geometry optimizations and frequency calculations for various metallated states (M2, M1, M0) will be performed using the B3LYP functional with the D3BJ dispersion correction and the def2-SVP basis set. Single-point energy refinements will use the def2-TZVP basis set. We will analyze electronic structure via Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) and Atoms-in-Molecules (AIM) theories, calculate electrostatic potential surfaces, and map reaction pathways for proposed catalytic cycles (e.g., dioxygen reduction) using the climbing-image nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantum-chemical blueprint of bimetallic cooperativity within a carbaporphyrin dimer, directly linking its unique architecture to function. It will predict optimal metal pairs and substrates for catalysis, offering testable hypotheses for subsequent synthesis. The study will establish a computational framework for analyzing metalated expanded porphyrins, bridging synthetic macrocyclic chemistry and theoretical inorganic chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is standard, its application to model the cooperative effects in a precisely synthesized, non-planar Janus carbaporphyrin dimer—a system merging anion receptor design with bimetallic site engineering—is unprecedented. The study moves beyond modeling simple porphyrins to address the complex, multi-functional systems pioneered by the Sessler group.

proposals.title: Spectroelectrochemical Mapping of Anion-Binding Induced Electronic Perturbations in Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimers and Related Anion Receptors
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work from the Sessler group has pioneered the synthesis of non-innocent, redox-active macrocycles like carbaporphyrins and expanded porphyrins that act as anion receptors. While anion binding affinities are known, the direct, real-time impact of anion coordination on the electronic structure and redox orbitals of these unique, π-extended systems remains poorly understood. Spectroelectrochemistry is the ideal tool to directly correlate binding events with electronic transitions and redox potentials, moving beyond thermodynamic measurements to mechanistic insight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anion binding to the pyrrolic NH sites of Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers and related receptors (e.g., calix[4]pyrroles, dipyrrolylquinoxalines) will induce quantifiable, anion-specific shifts in both their redox potentials and their characteristic electronic absorption bands, revealing the extent of charge transfer and orbital energy level modulation upon complexation.
proposals.method: We will employ in-situ UV-Vis-NIR spectroelectrochemistry using an optically transparent thin-layer electrochemical (OTTLE) cell. Target complexes will be studied in dry, degassed dichloromethane with [n-Bu4N][PF6] as electrolyte. Cyclic voltammetry will first identify redox potentials. Subsequently, controlled-potential bulk electrolysis at each redox couple will be performed while simultaneously recording full electronic spectra (300-1600 nm). This process will be repeated for each complex in its free state and when titrated with specific anions (e.g., F-, Cl-, H2PO4- as their tetrabutylammonium salts). Data analysis will focus on correlating Nernstian shifts in formal potential (E1/2) with the emergence, disappearance, or shifting of diagnostic absorption bands (e.g., porphyrinoid Soret, Q-bands, and near-IR intervals).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, spectroscopic 'fingerprint' of the anion-bound redox states of these sophisticated receptors, quantifying how different anions perturb the host's electronic structure. It will establish structure-property relationships linking macrocyclic architecture (e.g., carbaporphyrin vs. calixpyrrole) to the magnitude of anion-induced electrochemical and spectroscopic shifts, informing the design of new redox-switchable sensors or molecular devices.
proposals.novelty: While spectroelectrochemistry is established for porphyrins, its application to probe anion-binding events in their non-porphyrinoid, 'confused' carbaporphyrin analogs and related anion receptors is novel. The approach moves beyond observing binding constants to visualizing the real-time electronic consequences of anion recognition in these multifunctional systems.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of a Trinuclear Metal-Sapphyrin Complex
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins like sapphyrin offer larger, more flexible coordination cavities than porphyrins, enabling the binding of larger anions and multiple metal ions. Pre-2026 work has established sapphyrin's utility in anion sensing and transport, but discrete, well-defined complexes with three or more metal centers within a single sapphyrin framework remain unexplored. Such high-nuclearity complexes could create unique, multi-anion receptors with cooperative binding effects and novel magnetic or catalytic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the expanded core of a sapphyrin macrocycle can be rationally functionalized to coordinate three transition metal ions (e.g., Zn(II), Cu(II)), and that this trinuclear complex will exhibit enhanced and cooperative anion binding affinities compared to its mononuclear or dinuclear analogs.
proposals.method: The approach will involve the synthesis of a sapphyrin derivative pre-functionalized with specific metal-binding sites (e.g., β-pyrrolic substituents or core-modified heteroatoms). Metalation studies will be performed using acetate salts of Zn(II) and Cu(II) under controlled conditions. The resulting complexes will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Anion binding studies will be conducted via UV-Vis and fluorescence titration in organic solvents, focusing on fluoride, chloride, and phosphate anions, to quantify binding constants and assess cooperativity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new synthetic methodology for creating high-nuclearity expanded porphyrin complexes. It will provide the first detailed study of multi-anion recognition by a trinuclear sapphyrin receptor, potentially revealing positive cooperativity. The findings will advance the field of supramolecular coordination chemistry for multi-site substrate activation.
proposals.novelty: While Sessler's prior work includes carbaporphyrin dimers and anion receptors, moving to a trinuclear complex within a single sapphyrin scaffold is a significant escalation in nuclearity and complexity. The deliberate design of a sapphyrin-based multi-metal anion receptor with potential cooperative effects is unprecedented in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Core-Substituted Carbaporphyrinoids: Systematic Tuning of Anion Affinity and Selectivity via Tripyrrane Precursor Modification
proposals.motivation: Anion receptors based on expanded porphyrinoids, such as carbaporphyrins and calix[4]pyrroles, are crucial for sensing and transport applications, but their binding properties are often difficult to fine-tune. Pre-2026 work by Sessler and others has demonstrated that core structure dramatically influences anion affinity, yet a systematic study on the effect of electronic and steric modifications to the tripyrrane precursor of carbaporphyrinoids is lacking. This gap limits the rational design of next-generation anion receptors with tailored selectivity for biologically relevant anions like fluoride and chloride.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing electron-donating or electron-withdrawing substituents at the core of the tripyrrane precursor will predictably modulate the anion binding affinity and selectivity of the resulting carbaporphyrinoid macrocycles by altering the acidity of the pyrrolic NH groups and the overall electrostatic potential of the binding cavity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of 1,9-diphenyltripyrranes with varied substituents (e.g., -OMe, -F, -CF3, -CN) at the 5-position of the central pyrrole. These precursors will be used in established acid-catalyzed condensation reactions with appropriate diformyl derivatives to yield a library of core-substituted carbaporphyrinoid macrocycles. The compounds will be fully characterized (NMR, MS, X-ray). Anion binding studies will be conducted using UV-Vis and 1H NMR titration methods in dichloromethane/acetonitrile to determine stability constants (Ka) for fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-activity relationship between core substituents and anion binding in carbaporphyrinoids, providing a rational design blueprint. It will deliver a new family of tunable anion receptors with potentially unprecedented selectivity profiles, advancing the field of supramolecular anion recognition. The findings could directly inform the development of improved anion sensors or transmembrane transport agents.
proposals.novelty: While carbaporphyrin synthesis is established, systematic electronic tuning via the tripyrrane core is unexplored. This approach moves beyond peripheral modification, directly targeting the electronic structure of the binding pocket, a strategy distinct from prior work on calix[4]pyrrole or simple porphyrin-based receptors.

proposals.title: Solid-State Anion-Templated Assembly: A Comparative Structural Study of a Janus Carbaporphyrin Pseudo-Dimer with Bromide and Iodide
proposals.motivation: The Sessler group's recent synthesis of a Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer, which forms a chloride-templated dimeric capsule in the solid state, represents a breakthrough in anion-directed supramolecular assembly using expanded porphyrinoids. Pre-2026, understanding how anion size and polarizability influence the geometry and stability of such assemblies is critical for designing functional anion receptors and porous materials. A systematic comparison with larger halides (Br-, I-) is missing but essential to elucidate fundamental principles of anion templating.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the larger ionic radii and increased polarizability of bromide and iodide will induce distinct, predictable distortions in the macrocyclic framework and alter the dimeric capsule geometry compared to the chloride complex, potentially leading to changes in cavity size and intermolecular interactions.
proposals.method: The target Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimer will be synthesized following established pre-2026 procedures. Crystallization will be attempted via vapor diffusion or slow evaporation using tetrabutylammonium salts of bromide and iodide as the anion source in solvents like dichloromethane/hexanes. Single crystals suitable for X-ray diffraction will be obtained and their structures solved. The resulting solid-state structures will be meticulously compared to the known chloride complex using crystallographic software (e.g., Olex2, SHELX) to analyze metrics such as anion-N(pyrrole) distances, macrocyclic conformation, dimeric offset, and cavity dimensions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct structural comparison of halide binding within this novel carbaporphyrin scaffold, establishing a structure-property relationship for anion-templated assembly. It will contribute fundamental insights into the adaptability of expanded porphyrinoids as anion receptors and their potential for creating anion-specific solid-state architectures, informing future design of selective sensors or separation materials.
proposals.novelty: While anion binding in solution is well-studied, a targeted crystallographic investigation of a single, sophisticated macrocycle with a series of halides to directly visualize and compare binding geometries is novel. This moves beyond simple solution-phase affinity measurements to reveal the precise structural consequences of anion exchange in a pre-organized, dimeric system.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Quinoxaline-Pyrrole Anion Sensors via Combinatorial Synthesis and Fluoride-Triggered Fluorescence Screening
proposals.motivation: Anion sensing, particularly for environmentally and biologically relevant species like fluoride and chloride, remains a critical challenge in supramolecular chemistry. While Sessler's work on 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxalines and calix[4]pyrroles has established foundational motifs for anion binding, traditional one-at-a-time synthesis is slow for optimizing selectivity and sensitivity. A combinatorial approach is needed to rapidly map structure-activity relationships and discover superior sensors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic, high-throughput variation of electron-withdrawing and steric substituents on the pyrrole and quinoxaline cores will yield novel compounds with significantly magnified anion-binding affinities and fluorescence turn-on responses, particularly for fluoride, which can be efficiently identified via parallel screening.
proposals.method: We will employ solid-phase parallel synthesis to generate a library of >200 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxaline derivatives, varying substituents (e.g., -F, -CF3, -CN, ester groups) on the pyrrole β-positions and quinoxaline ring. Screening will utilize 96-well plate fluorescence spectroscopy, monitoring emission intensity changes upon addition of tetrabutylammonium salts of F-, Cl-, H2PO4-, and HSO4- in acetonitrile/DMSO mixtures. Primary hits will be characterized by UV-Vis titration and 1H NMR to determine binding constants and stoichiometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a curated dataset linking specific substituent patterns to anion selectivity and binding strength for the quinoxaline-pyrrole scaffold, establishing design rules for future sensors. It will deliver one or more lead compounds with sub-micromolar fluoride sensitivity and a demonstrated, practical high-throughput discovery pipeline for supramolecular chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While combinatorial methods are common in drug discovery, their application to the systematic optimization of anion-responsive π-conjugated systems like dipyrrolylquinoxalines is novel. This approach moves beyond synthesizing single, designed molecules to empirically exploring a chemical space for emergent, high-performance sensor properties.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Hybrid Expanded Porphyrinoids: Replacing Pyrrole with Thiophene and Furan Units
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins and carbaporphyrins, pioneered by the Sessler group, are powerful platforms for anion recognition and transport, as seen in calix[4]pyrroles and dipyrrolylquinoxalines. However, the fundamental role of the pyrrole NH unit in anion binding via hydrogen bonding imposes limits on redox stability, electronic structure, and binding selectivity. Systematic replacement of pyrrole with other heterocycles remains an underexplored route to modulate these properties while retaining macrocyclic architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selectively replacing one or more pyrrole units in expanded porphyrinoid frameworks with thiophene or furan rings will yield stable hybrid macrocycles with distinct electronic properties, altered anion-binding affinities, and new selectivity profiles, moving beyond purely pyrrole-based receptors.
proposals.method: We will employ a modular, acid-catalyzed condensation strategy, analogous to porphyrin synthesis, using diformyl derivatives of thiophene and furan alongside traditional pyrrolic dialdehydes or dipyrromethanes. Target macrocycles include hybrid [22]π-hexaphyrins and carbaporphyrin analogues. Products will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Anion binding studies will be conducted via UV-Vis and 1H NMR titration in organic solvents (e.g., acetonitrile) with tetrabutylammonium salts of fluoride, chloride, and dihydrogen phosphate to quantify affinity and selectivity changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new family of hybrid expanded porphyrinoids, mapping how heterocycle identity dictates macrocycle conformation, aromaticity, and anion receptor function. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships essential for designing next-generation sensors and transmembrane transporters with tailored properties.
proposals.novelty: While heterocycle replacement in small molecules is known, its systematic application to large, functional expanded porphyrinoid scaffolds to directly perturb the core anion-binding site is novel, moving beyond simple peripheral substitution or carbasubstitution.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Expanded Carbaporphyrinoids Incorporating Oxygen, Sulfur, and Fused Polycyclic Aromatic Units
proposals.motivation: The field of porphyrinoids has progressed beyond classic tetrapyrroles to include expanded macrocycles and core-modified systems like carbaporphyrins, which exhibit unique coordination and anion-binding properties. However, the systematic expansion of the macrocyclic framework with heteroatoms other than nitrogen (e.g., O, S) or the integration of fused aromatic panels remains largely unexplored. Developing such architectures is crucial for accessing novel electronic structures, enhanced anion affinities, and unprecedented supramolecular functions beyond the scope of traditional nitrogen-rich porphyrin analogs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing pyrrolic nitrogen atoms with oxygen or sulfur within an expanded carbaporphyrinoid framework, and/or fusing extended aromatic systems to the core, will dramatically alter the macrocycle's electronic properties, cavity size, and anion-binding selectivity, particularly for fluoride and chloride, compared to known all-nitrogen expanded porphyrins.
proposals.method: The synthesis will employ a modular, stepwise approach inspired by Sessler's carbaporphyrin and dimer work. First, tailored precursors containing furan, thiophene, or naphthalene/phenanthrene-fused pyrrole units will be synthesized. These will be coupled under modified Lindsey-type conditions to form expanded macrocycles (e.g., pentaphyrins, hexaphyrins). Key steps will include template-assisted macrocyclization and subsequent oxidation/aromatization. The products will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Anion binding will be evaluated via UV-Vis/fluorescence titration and 1H/19F NMR in organic solvents, focusing on fluoride and chloride, benchmarked against octafluorocalix[4]pyrrole and dipyrrolylquinoxaline-based receptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of heteroatom-doped and π-extended expanded porphyrinoids, mapping the relationship between core heteroatom identity, aromatic topology, and anion receptor capability. It will provide fundamental insights into macrocyclic aromaticity and antiaromaticity in non-tetrapyrrole systems. The project will deliver novel, tunable scaffolds for advanced sensing, transport, and materials applications where specific anion recognition is critical.
proposals.novelty: While expanded porphyrins and carbaporphyrins are known, the deliberate incorporation of oxygen/sulfur heterocycles into their expanded frameworks, combined with strategic fusion of polycyclic aromatic units, represents a significant and unexplored departure from traditional porphyrinoid chemistry, moving beyond simple ring expansion or nitrogen substitution.

proposals.title: Mapping Excited-State Dynamics in Carbaporphyrin Dimers and Anion Complexes via Ultrafast Spectroscopy and Single-Molecule Imaging
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins, including carbaporphyrin dimers, exhibit rich photophysics crucial for applications in sensing, photonics, and photodynamic therapy. However, their excited-state dynamics, especially in response to anion binding as pioneered by Sessler's group, remain poorly characterized compared to conventional organic dyes. Applying the sophisticated photophysical toolkit developed for dyes to these complex macrocycles will reveal the fundamental photophysical consequences of structural distortion and anion recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unique structural features of carbaporphyrin dimers and their anion complexes will manifest in distinct, quantifiable excited-state pathways—including charge transfer, intersystem crossing, and excimer formation—that can be decoded using advanced time-resolved and single-molecule techniques.
proposals.method: We will synthesize target systems: Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers and their complexes with anions like fluoride. Photophysical characterization will employ (1) ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-µs) to map excited-state kinetics and identify intermediates, (2) time-resolved fluorescence upconversion for early singlet-state dynamics, and (3) single-molecule fluorescence microscopy (including burst analysis and defocused imaging) to probe heterogeneity, blinking, and rotational dynamics in individual macrocycles, techniques rarely applied to such systems pre-2026.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a detailed photophysical map linking the non-planar, dimeric structures of carbaporphyrins to their functional excited-state behavior. It will directly quantify how anion binding modulates photophysics, providing a foundation for designing porphyrinoid-based sensors and materials with tailored light-responsive properties. The application of single-molecule methods will reveal static and dynamic disorder not accessible via ensemble measurements.
proposals.novelty: While Sessler's group has excelled in synthesizing and demonstrating anion binding in these systems, a systematic, state-of-the-art photophysical investigation combining ultrafast and single-molecule methodologies represents a significant, non-obvious advancement beyond routine fluorescence quantum yield measurements.

proposals.title: Non-Planar Carbacorrphycen Dimers as Versatile Anion Receptors: Challenging the Planarity Paradigm in Expanded Porphyrinoid Coordination
proposals.motivation: In expanded porphyrinoid chemistry, planarity is often considered essential for effective metal ion and anion binding, as it facilitates optimal orbital overlap and preorganization. However, the Sessler group's pioneering work on carbaporphyrins and their dimers (e.g., Janus carbaporphyrin pseudo-dimers) demonstrates that controlled distortion can create unique, preorganized cavities. This proposal seeks to systematically test whether deliberate, non-planar distortions in carbacorrphycen scaffolds can be leveraged to create novel anion receptors, moving beyond the planar constraint.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically designed, non-planar carbacorrphycen dimers can act as effective and selective anion receptors, where the distortion itself creates a preorganized three-dimensional binding pocket that enhances affinity and selectivity for target anions, particularly fluoride and chloride.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of carbacorrphycen monomers with sterically bulky substituents (e.g., mesityl groups) at key meso- and β-positions to enforce non-planarity. These will be linked via flexible or rigid spacers to form dimers, creating distorted, bowl-shaped cavities. The anion binding properties (affinity, selectivity) will be evaluated using standard pre-2026 techniques: UV-Vis and fluorescence titration studies in organic solvents (e.g., acetonitrile, DCM) with tetrabutylammonium salts of F-, Cl-, H2PO4-, and HSO4-. Binding constants will be determined via non-linear regression analysis. Structural confirmation will be obtained via X-ray crystallography of anion complexes where possible.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for anion receptors based on non-planar, distorted porphyrinoid frameworks, expanding the toolbox beyond planar macrocycles and calixpyrroles. It will provide fundamental insights into how controlled distortion influences host-guest chemistry, potentially leading to receptors with unprecedented selectivity profiles for challenging anions like fluoride.
proposals.novelty: While anion binding by planar expanded porphyrins is known, the intentional use of enforced non-planarity in carbacorrphycen dimers to create anion-binding cavities is unexplored. This directly challenges the dogma that effective complexation requires a planar, conjugated platform.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Partially Saturated and Conjugation-Broken Corrphycen Analogues
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrins, like corrphycen, offer unique anion-binding cavities but are often rigid and synthetically challenging due to their fully conjugated, aromatic macrocycles. Relaxing the requirement for full conjugation could yield more flexible, synthetically accessible, and tunable macrocycles. This would allow systematic exploration of how electronic structure and conformational dynamics influence anion recognition, a core theme in the Sessler group's work on anion receptors like calix[4]pyrroles and dipyrrolylquinoxalines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategic reduction of double bonds or insertion of sp3-hybridized linkers within the corrphycen skeleton will yield stable, non-aromatic macrocycles with enhanced flexibility and distinct anion-binding profiles compared to their fully conjugated counterparts.
proposals.method: We will synthesize target macrocycles via modified Lindsey-type condensations, using dihydro- or tetrahydro-dipyrromethane precursors to introduce saturated units. Key steps include acid-catalyzed macrocyclization followed by careful oxidation control to preserve the desired saturation. The products will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Their anion-binding properties (affinity, selectivity) will be quantified via UV-Vis and 1H NMR titration studies in organic solvents, focusing on fluoride and chloride, benchmarked against known aromatic corrphycen and calix[4]pyrrole receptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of tunable anion receptors derived from the corrphycen scaffold, demonstrating that high anion affinity does not strictly require a fully conjugated, aromatic platform. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships linking macrocycle saturation, conformation, and anion-binding efficacy, enriching the toolbox for designing selective transmembrane anion transporters.
proposals.novelty: While carbaporphyrins and core-modified porphyrins are known, the deliberate, systematic breaking of conjugation in the corrphycen framework to modulate anion binding is unexplored. This moves beyond simple peripheral substitution to core architectural modification, merging concepts from non-aromatic calixpyrrole anion receptors with the expanded porphyrin skeleton.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of Peripherally Modified Corrphycen Derivatives
proposals.motivation: Corrphycen, a carbaporphyrinoid with a direct pyrrole-pyrrole linkage, exhibits a unique, non-aromatic, yet highly conjugated structure with a pre-organized cavity. Prior to 2026, its synthesis and fundamental properties have been established, but systematic modification to tune its electronic structure and host-guest capabilities remains unexplored. This project aims to leverage the Sessler group's expertise in expanded porphyrins and anion receptors to develop a new class of tunable macrocyclic anion hosts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing electron-donating or electron-withdrawing alkyl and aryl substituents at the peripheral positions of the corrphycen framework will systematically modulate its electron density, cavity polarity, and conformational flexibility, thereby enhancing and diversifying its anion-binding affinity and selectivity, particularly for fluoride and chloride.
proposals.method: We will employ a modular synthesis strategy, adapting established corrphycen-forming reactions (e.g., acid-catalyzed condensation of tailored diformyl-dipyrromethanes with appropriate tripyrrane analogues). Key synthetic targets will include derivatives bearing pentafluorophenyl (electron-withdrawing), mesityl (sterically hindered), and simple alkyl (electron-donating) groups. The anion-binding properties will be evaluated using standard pre-2026 techniques: UV-Vis and fluorescence titration studies in organic solvents (e.g., acetonitrile, dichloromethane) to determine binding constants (Ka) for halides (F-, Cl-, Br-, I-) and oxoanions (e.g., H2PO4-), complemented by 1H NMR titration experiments to probe binding-induced conformational changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-property relationships for corrphycen-based anion receptors, demonstrating how peripheral substitution can be used as a powerful tool to 'dial-in' desired binding characteristics. It will expand the library of synthetically accessible carbaporphyrinoids and provide new fundamental insights into the design of non-aromatic macrocyclic hosts for anion recognition and transport.
proposals.novelty: While the Sessler group has pioneered carbaporphyrin dimers and anion receptors like calix[4]pyrroles, applying systematic peripheral functionalization to the distinct, non-aromatic corrphycen core to create a new anion receptor family is a novel and logical extension of their pre-2026 work, moving beyond simple characterization to targeted property engineering.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Characterization of Metal-Corrphycen Complexes: Exploring Coordination Chemistry in a Carbaporphyrinoid Framework
proposals.motivation: Corrphycen, a carbaporphyrinoid where one pyrrole is replaced by a cyclopentadiene unit, presents a unique, non-innocent ligand scaffold with an expanded, potentially flexible core. While the Sessler group has pioneered carbaporphyrin chemistry and anion receptors like calix[4]pyrroles, the systematic exploration of corrphycen's metal-binding properties remains underdeveloped. Understanding its coordination behavior is crucial for developing new metallo-expanded porphyrins with applications in catalysis, sensing, and materials science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that corrphycen will act as a versatile, monoanionic ligand capable of forming stable, structurally distinct complexes with a range of transition and main group metals, and that its non-porphyrinoid cavity will induce unusual metal oxidation states and coordination geometries compared to traditional porphyrins.
proposals.method: We will synthesize corrphycen using established routes. Metalation will be performed under inert conditions using metal salts (e.g., Ni(II), Cu(II), Pd(II), Ag(I), Sn(IV), In(III)). Complexes will be purified via column chromatography and characterized by 1H/19F NMR, high-resolution mass spectrometry, UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopy, and X-ray crystallography. Electrochemical properties will be probed via cyclic voltammetry. Anion binding studies (e.g., with fluoride) on selected metallo-corrphycens will assess modulated receptor properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the foundational coordination chemistry of corrphycen, mapping its affinity and structural preferences across the periodic table. It will deliver a new library of metallo-carbaporphyrinoids with characterized electronic and redox properties. The study may reveal complexes with applications as novel anion sensors or catalysts, bridging Sessler's expertise in anion recognition and expanded porphyrins.
proposals.novelty: While metal complexes of porphyrins and some carbaporphyrins are known, a systematic study focused on the distinct corrphycen scaffold is unprecedented. The investigation integrates metal coordination chemistry with the group's recent focus on anion receptors, exploring how metalation alters the macrocycle's host-guest properties.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Stability and Redox Non-Innocence in a Corrphycen Macrocycle
proposals.motivation: Corrphycen, a contracted porphyrinoid with a direct pyrrole-pyrrole linkage, represents a unique structural perturbation to the classic porphyrin framework. Pre-2026 work from the Sessler group on carbaporphyrins and their dimers has demonstrated that core modification dramatically alters electronic structure and anion binding. The fundamental electrochemical properties and chemical robustness of the corrphycen core, however, remain unexplored, limiting its application in catalysis, sensing, and materials science where redox activity and stability are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the contracted, non-aromatic corrphycen core will exhibit distinct, multi-step redox behavior distinct from porphyrins and carbaporphyrins, and that its stability under oxidative and reductive conditions is linked to its non-innocent ligand character and propensity for proton-coupled electron transfer.
proposals.method: The study will employ cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry in aprotic (e.g., CH2Cl2/TBAPF6) and protic solvents to map redox potentials and assess reversibility. Spectroelectrochemistry (UV-Vis-NIR) will be used to monitor the generation and characterize the electronic structures of oxidized and reduced species in situ. Chemical stability will be probed via bulk electrolysis at defined potentials followed by analysis using 1H NMR and mass spectrometry to identify decomposition products. Anion binding studies (e.g., with fluoride or chloride) will be conducted electrochemically to assess modulation of redox potentials by anion receptor interactions, leveraging the group's expertise in pyrrole-based anion receptors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive redox map and stability profile for the corrphycen class, providing critical design parameters for its use in molecular electronics and as a redox-active ligand or catalyst. It will directly connect macrocyclic contraction and non-aromaticity to electrochemical behavior, expanding the toolbox of tunable porphyrinoid platforms.
proposals.novelty: While the redox chemistry of porphyrins and expanded porphyrins is well-known, the electrochemical interrogation of the contracted, directly linked corrphycen scaffold is unprecedented. The explicit investigation of its stability under redox conditions and potential redox non-innocence moves beyond simple property measurement to mechanistic understanding.

proposals.title: Supramolecular Templating of a Carbaporphyrin-Corrphycene Dimer via Anion-Directed Macrocyclization
proposals.motivation: Corrphycenes, contracted porphyrinoids with distinct optical and metal-binding properties, are challenging to synthesize in high yields due to entropic penalties during macrocyclization. Pre-2026, the Sessler group has pioneered carbaporphyrin chemistry and anion-responsive systems, demonstrating that supramolecular pre-organization can guide synthesis. Leveraging this expertise to template corrphycene formation addresses a key synthetic bottleneck in porphyrinoid chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bifunctional carbaporphyrin monomer, functionalized with anion-binding sites (e.g., pyrrolylquinoxaline or calixpyrrole motifs), will pre-organize around a suitable anionic template (e.g., fluoride or chloride), thereby facilitating intramolecular ring closure to form a novel carbaporphyrin-corrphycene dimer in a controlled, templated manner.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a linear tetrapyrrolic precursor bearing a carbaporphyrin unit at one terminus and a reactive diformyl-dipyrromethane at the other, linked via a conformationally flexible bridge. Key anion receptor units (e.g., 2,3-dipyrrolylquinoxaline) will be incorporated near the reactive ends. Macrocyclization will be attempted under high-dilution conditions both with and without templating anions (F-, Cl-, H2PO4-). Reaction progress will be monitored by HPLC and UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy. The templated product will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography, with anion binding affinity of the final dimer quantified via titration experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel supramolecular strategy for synthesizing contracted porphyrinoids, directly yielding a new class of anion-responsive chromophore dimers. It will provide fundamental insights into templated macrocyclization dynamics and deliver a unique platform with potential applications in anion sensing and transport, building on the group's prior work with antiport agents.
proposals.novelty: While anion-templated synthesis of catenanes and calixarenes is known, applying this method to the kinetically challenging formation of a corrphycene ring, particularly as part of a heterodimer with a carbaporphyrin, is unprecedented. The approach merges the group's distinct expertise in carbaporphyrin dimers and anion receptors in a novel synthetic context.

proposals.title: Electrochemically Driven Macrocyclization for the Synthesis of Carbaporphyrinoids and Corrphycen Analogues
proposals.motivation: Traditional syntheses of contracted porphyrinoids like corrphycen and carbaporphyrins often require harsh oxidative conditions, high temperatures, or stoichiometric oxidants, limiting functional group tolerance and yields. Electrochemical synthesis offers a powerful, green alternative by using controlled electron transfer to drive macrocyclization under mild, tunable conditions, potentially enabling access to novel, functionalized macrocycles. This approach aligns with the Sessler group's expertise in porphyrinoid synthesis and anion-responsive macrocycles.
proposals.hypothesis: Electrochemical oxidation of appropriately functionalized dipyrromethane or oligopyrrolic precursors will facilitate a controlled, intramolecular C-C or C-N bond-forming macrocyclization, providing a general route to corrphycen and related carbaporphyrinoid scaffolds under ambient conditions. The electrochemical potential can be finely tuned to match the oxidation potential of the precursor, minimizing over-oxidation and side reactions.
proposals.method: Precursors will be designed based on known corrphycen syntheses, featuring terminal pyrroles or other heterocycles with electron-rich sites for coupling. Cyclic voltammetry will first be used to determine the oxidation potentials of these precursors. Constant-potential electrolysis will then be performed in anhydrous, degassed dichloromethane or acetonitrile with a supporting electrolyte (e.g., tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate), using a divided cell with platinum or glassy carbon electrodes. Reaction progress will be monitored by HPLC and UV-vis spectroscopy, with products isolated via column chromatography and characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, mild electrochemical method for synthesizing contracted porphyrinoids, expanding the toolbox for macrocyclic chemistry. Successfully applying this to corrphycen will demonstrate its utility for challenging ring-contracted systems. The resulting electrochemically synthesized macrocycles can be immediately evaluated as novel anion receptors, leveraging the group's established work on fluoride and chloride sensing, potentially leading to receptors with unique binding affinities or electrochemical sensing capabilities.
proposals.novelty: While electrochemistry has been used in porphyrin synthesis, its application to the specific, challenging macrocyclization forming ring-contracted systems like corrphycen is unexplored. Combining the Sessler group's deep knowledge of carbaporphyrinoid synthesis and anion recognition with modern electrochemical methods represents a novel, convergent approach to functional macrocycle fabrication.

proposals.title: Contracted Corrphycen Analogues: Synthesis and Anion-Binding Properties of N-Confused Tripyrrin and Corrole Dimers
proposals.motivation: Expanded porphyrinoids like carbaporphyrins and corrphycenes exhibit unique, often enhanced anion binding properties due to their distorted, electron-rich cavities. However, their synthesis is typically complex and their size can limit structural control. Contracting the macrocycle core, as seen in corroles and tripyrrins, could offer a more synthetically accessible route to highly pre-organized, tunable anion receptors with potentially magnified affinities and selectivities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that N-confused tripyrrin and corrole dimers, which mimic the connectivity pattern of corrphycen but with a contracted core, will form rigid, concave cavities that act as potent and selective receptors for fluoride and other small anions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize target dimers via acid-catalyzed condensations of diformyl N-confused pyrroles with appropriate dipyrromethanes. The tripyrrin-based target will be a directly linked dimer, while the corrole-based target will utilize a quinoxaline or phenylene bridge, inspired by Sessler's previous work on dipyrrolylquinoxaline sensors. The compounds will be characterized by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography. Anion binding will be evaluated via UV-Vis, fluorescence, and 1H NMR titration studies in organic solvents (e.g., acetonitrile, DMSO) to determine association constants (Ka) for fluoride, chloride, and bicarbonate.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of contracted, pre-organized anion receptors derived from corrphycen connectivity, expanding the structural lexicon of anion supramolecular chemistry. It will provide fundamental structure-property relationships linking core contraction, cavity pre-organization, and anion affinity/selectivity, particularly for challenging targets like fluoride.
proposals.novelty: While corroles and tripyrrins are known, their deliberate assembly into dimers that replicate the core geometry of larger, more complex systems like corrphycen for anion recognition is unexplored. This represents a 'scale-down' strategy to achieve potent function from simpler, more tractable building blocks.

ideas.title: Systematic Screening of Expanded Porphyrin Cavities via a Materials Genome Approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a six-pyrrole unit represents an optimal cavity size by drawing an analogy from catalysis, where optimal pore size is discovered empirically. In zeolite and MOF research, high-throughput synthesis and screening of materials with systematically varied pore sizes is used to find the best structure for a given catalytic function. Adapted to porphyrin chemistry, this involves synthesizing a comprehensive library of expanded porphyrins (e.g., from tetra- to decapyrrole) and screening them against a panel of key performance metrics—such as metal ion binding constants, photophysical properties, or catalytic turnover numbers. This data-driven, combinatorial approach will empirically map cavity size to function, likely revealing multiple 'optimal' sizes for different applications and definitively testing the six-pyrrule paradigm.

ideas.title: Establishing a calibrated multi-metric aromaticity scale for rubyrin and related porphyrinoids
ideas.description: This study first defines a quantitative aromaticity scale by benchmarking a suite of computational and experimental metrics against a curated series of reference macrocycles with established aromatic, non-aromatic, and anti-aromatic character. Rubyrin is then rigorously positioned on this scale through integrated DFT calculations, NMR spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction analysis, and magnetically-induced current measurements. The core innovation is the creation of a validated, weighted scoring system that resolves conflicts between different aromaticity indicators, providing a definitive and transferable answer for rubyrin's electronic structure. This framework transforms a single-molecule inquiry into a general tool for classifying the electronic nature of complex porphyrinoid systems.

ideas.title: Computationally-guided synthesis of metastable protonation states for targeted property enhancement
ideas.description: This project first employs high-throughput DFT and ab initio molecular dynamics to screen candidate molecules and identify those where non-standard protonation states (e.g., deprotonated or multiply protonated) are energetically accessible and potentially stable within specific chemical environments, such as non-aqueous solvents or macrocyclic hosts. The computational workflow predicts key spectroscopic signatures (NMR, IR) and target properties (redox potential, band gap) for these states. Subsequently, guided synthesis is performed in the predicted stabilizing media, and the generated species are characterized using in situ spectroelectrochemical and cryogenic spectroscopic techniques to validate the formation and properties of the targeted protonation isomer.

ideas.title: Quantify Supramolecular Recognition Dynamics of Large Anions and Fullerenes with Rubyrin Hosts
ideas.description: This research direction combines the structural host-guest chemistry of rubyrin macrocycles with quantitative kinetic and thermodynamic analysis. It involves synthesizing a series of rubyrin derivatives with systematically varied electronic and steric properties to act as hosts for large anions (e.g., polyoxometalates, anionic metal complexes) and fullerenes. Using techniques like isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), stopped-flow spectroscopy, and variable-temperature NMR, the binding constants (Ka), enthalpy/entropy contributions (ΔH, ΔS), and association/dissociation rate constants (k_on, k_off) for these complexes will be rigorously determined. The resulting dataset will establish structure-dynamics-property relationships, guiding the design of rubyrin-based systems for applications in molecular recognition, sequestration, and sensing where both high affinity and tunable binding kinetics are critical.

ideas.title: Probing the Catalytic Competence of Rubyrin-Metal Complexes for Small Molecule Activation
ideas.description: This project moves beyond simple metal binding to investigate the functional catalytic potential of rubyrin-metal complexes. We will synthesize and characterize rubyrin complexes with redox-active metals (e.g., Fe, Co, Ni) and rigorously test their ability to stabilize reactive intermediates. The core activity will be evaluating these complexes as electrocatalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) or oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), thereby directly linking rubyrin's unique coordination environment to a measurable and significant chemical function.

ideas.title: Functionalize figure-eight conformations for chirality

ideas.title: Decoupling Geometry and Conjugation: Testing Aromaticity in Rigid, Non-Planar Möbius Macrocycles
ideas.description: This research directly addresses the ambiguity in the original idea by designing synthetically rigidified, non-planar macrocyclic π-systems that enforce a single, stable Möbius-twisted conformation. These molecules maintain a continuous, conjugated pathway for electron delocalization despite their non-planarity, cleanly isolating the variable of global molecular shape. Their aromatic character is then rigorously assessed using nucleus-independent chemical shift (NICS) calculations, anisotropy of the induced current density (ACID) plots, and experimental methods like 1H NMR chemical shifts to determine if sustained diamagnetic ring currents—the hallmark of aromaticity—can exist in a definitively non-planar framework.

ideas.title: Janus-Type Molecular Probe for Simultaneous Cation and Anion Detection
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from supramolecular chemistry's Janus-type molecules, which feature two distinct functional faces. The analogous solution involves designing a single molecular scaffold with spatially separated, dedicated binding sites for opposite purposes. Adapted to sensor design, the idea is to create a Janus-type molecular probe with a rigid core. One side of the molecule will incorporate a cation-binding site (e.g., a crown ether derivative), while the opposite side will incorporate an anion-binding site (e.g., a thiourea group). This dual-function, single-probe architecture allows for the simultaneous or independent detection of cationic and anionic species, enabling ionic pair analysis through integrated spectroscopic signal changes.

ideas.title: Question the six-pyrrole unit as optimal size
ideas.description: Synthesize heptapyrrole or pentapyrrole expanded porphyrins to find an optimal cavity size.

ideas.title: Assume non-identical porphyrin excitation energies
ideas.description: Use porphyrins with different substituents to create an energy gradient within the trimer.

ideas.title: Pre-organized, Rigid Scaffolds with Synergistic Binding to Amplify Through-Space Anion-Responsive Signals
ideas.description: This project designs and synthesizes structurally rigid, shape-persistent molecular frameworks that lock weakly conjugated or non-conjugated chromophoric units into a fixed geometry optimal for through-space interactions with a target anion. A primary, geometrically constrained binding site (e.g., a hydrogen-bonding cleft) is integrated to position the anion precisely within the interaction zone of the chromophore. A secondary, cooperative binding motif (e.g., electrostatic or dipole) is added to enhance binding affinity and selectivity. This combined approach ensures that the inherently weak through-space perturbation is translated into a strong, reproducible optical signal shift, enabling the detection of anions in aqueous or high-competition environments without relying on extended conjugation.

ideas.title: Investigate inverse problem: cation binding
ideas.description: Modify the macrocycle to a deprotonated, anionic form and study its binding properties for metal cations.

ideas.title: Extend sensor testing to diverse organic anions
ideas.description: Test the synthesized sensors against a broader panel of biologically relevant organic anions beyond acetate.

ideas.title: Study sensor-anion binding kinetics and thermodynamics
ideas.description: Quantitatively determine binding constants and rates for the unique anion responses observed.

ideas.title: Assume cation co-transport, not just metathesis
ideas.description: Design receptors that co-transport alkali metal cations with anions instead of requiring separate cation binders.

ideas.title: Invert the trapezoidal N4 site requirement

ideas.title: Explore metal coordination chemistry of rubyrin
ideas.description: Test rubyrin's ability to bind metal ions within its expanded macrocyclic cavity.

ideas.title: Optimize structure for specific anion selectivity
ideas.description: Systematically modify pyrrole and quinone substituents to enhance selectivity for pyrophosphate over phosphate.

ideas.title: Establish a predictive QSAR model for anion sensor performance via strategic chemical space mapping
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond simple panel testing to establish a predictive framework for sensor performance. A strategically curated library of organic anions, selected to span key structural and electronic parameters, is used to generate comprehensive binding data. This experimental data is integrated with computational chemistry descriptors for each anion to develop a quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) model. The resulting model identifies the dominant molecular features governing sensor selectivity and affinity, enabling the prediction of sensor response to novel, untested anions and guiding the rational design of next-generation receptors.

ideas.title: Scale complexity to multi-cavity cascade systems
ideas.description: Synthesize oligomeric or dendritic structures with multiple macrocyclic units for cooperative anion binding.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ein nichtaromatisches, von Anthracen abgeleitetes expandiertes Porphyrin — ein Makrocyclus, der Anionen bindet

ideas.title: Orthogonal-signal, spatially isolated dual-ion probe for independent cation and anion quantification
ideas.description: This redesigned sensor features two structurally independent and non-communicating binding domains—one for cations and one for anions—linked by a rigid spacer to prevent allosteric or electronic interference. Each domain is coupled to a dedicated, spectrally distinct fluorescent reporter, enabling simultaneous but independent detection of both ions through separate emission channels. This design allows for the accurate quantification of ionic pairs even in complex mixtures by eliminating signal crosstalk and binding site competition. The probe's performance is validated by demonstrating that the binding constant and fluorescence response for one ion remain unchanged regardless of the presence or absence of the other target ion.

ideas.title: Programmable Anion Selectivity via Dynamic Protonation State Switching
ideas.description: This research direction designs and synthesizes pyrrole-quinone-based receptors where the substituents are optimized not for a single protonation state, but to create a delicate energetic landscape for proton transfer. The core hypothesis is that the binding of pyrophosphate, with its specific charge density and geometry, will selectively stabilize a unique deprotonated or multiply protonated state of the receptor that is inaccessible or unfavorable when phosphate binds. This approach moves beyond static pre-organization, creating a system where anion binding and receptor protonation are cooperatively linked, leading to a highly specific binding event with an amplified optical or electrochemical output signal tied to the proton transfer.

ideas.title: Apply surface immobilization techniques from heterogeneous catalysis
ideas.description: Anchor the sapphyrin complex on a solid support to create a heterogeneous hydrogenation catalyst.

ideas.title: Dual-Parameter Photophysical Fingerprinting for Specific Anion Sensing in Complex Environments
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a single molecular sensor engineered to exhibit two orthogonal photophysical responses upon anion binding: a shift in fluorescence lifetime and a concurrent, measurable change in fluorescence anisotropy or emission wavelength ratio. These dual outputs are captured simultaneously via modified FLIM instrumentation. The correlated changes create a unique binding signature in a two-dimensional parameter space, allowing the specific anion-binding event to be spatially resolved and distinguished from false signals caused by local viscosity, temperature, or pH. This multi-parameter fingerprinting dramatically increases specificity and robustness for quantifying anion distributions in live cells or heterogeneous materials.

ideas.title: Use non-pyrrole heterocycles in expansion

ideas.description: Use molecular dynamics to simulate anion binding and translocation through the receptor's cavity.

ideas.title: Non-Planar Aromaticity via Topological Insulator Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics, specifically the field of topological insulators. In that field, the solved problem was demonstrating that conductive states could be protected by topological order rather than by the material's perfect crystalline structure. The analogous solution involved creating materials where electronic properties were decoupled from strict geometric constraints. Adapted to the chemistry context, this inspires an investigation into whether aromaticity in molecules like rubyrin can be a 'topologically protected' property of the cyclic π-conjugation pathway itself. The research would involve the design and synthesis of deliberately non-planar, twisted rubyrin analogs—potentially through steric hindrance or strategic bridging—to rigorously test if the aromatic character (measured via NMR ring currents, magnetic anisotropy, and energetic stabilization) persists when the traditional requirement of geometric planarity is violated. This challenges a foundational assumption in aromaticity theory by probing if the property is more fundamentally linked to the topology of the electron system than to molecular geometry.

ideas.title: Measure detailed photophysical properties
ideas.description: Conduct a full spectroscopic study of absorption, emission, and excited-state dynamics of Turcasarin.
ideas.sourceTitle: Turcasarin, das bisher am stärksten expandierte Porphyrin

ideas.title: Supramolecular Through-Space Signaling: Rubyrin Hosts for Anion Detection via Non-Conjugated Interactions
ideas.description: This research direction designs rubyrin-based macrocycles as supramolecular hosts specifically for large, bulky anions or anion-paired guest complexes. The key innovation is that anion binding within the rubyrin cavity will be transduced into a signal not through strong conjugation in the rubyrin backbone, but via through-space interactions such as intermolecular charge transfer, exciton coupling, or perturbation of aggregation state with intentionally attenuated chromophore units attached to the host periphery. This approach decouples the selectivity of the large, shape-persistent cavity from the constraints of a strongly conjugated signaling pathway, enabling the creation of sensors for supramolecular anions like metal clusters or organic anion assemblies that are currently inaccessible to conventional designs.

ideas.title: Solid-State Generation and Characterization of the Unsubstituted Rubyrin via Designed Precursor Dealkylation
ideas.description: This project overcomes the fatal insolubility of the target compound by first synthesizing a fully characterized, soluble tetraalkylrubyrin. This precursor is then deposited as a thin film or monolayer on an inert surface. A controlled thermal or chemical treatment is applied to cleave the alkyl groups in the solid state, directly yielding the insoluble, unsubstituted parent rubyrin framework immobilized on the surface. The generated material is then characterized using a suite of surface-sensitive and solid-state techniques to confirm the structure of the base rubyrin without requiring its dissolution.

ideas.title: Determine dimer structure via crystallography
ideas.description: Obtain X-ray crystal structures of the anion-bridged dimeric assemblies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bindung von Anionen: Selbstorganisation von Polypyrrolmakrocyclen†

ideas.title: Employ electrochemistry for anion sensing
ideas.description: Integrate the receptor into an electrode to create potentiometric anion sensors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ein nichtaromatisches, von Anthracen abgeleitetes expandiertes Porphyrin — ein Makrocyclus, der Anionen bindet

ideas.title: Supramolecular encapsulation of the DPQ motif in a engineered porous host for stable, reusable solid-state sensing
ideas.description: This redesigned approach focuses on preserving the solution-state performance of the dipyrrolylquinoxaline-quinone (DPQ) sensor motif in a solid-state format by avoiding direct covalent linkage. The DPQ derivative is non-covalently encapsulated within the engineered nano-pores of a robust, structured host material, such as a mesoporous silica or a tailored metal-organic framework. This supramolecular confinement maintains the motif's essential redox activity and binding geometry while providing a solid, reusable device platform. The resulting composite material enables repeated analyte sensing with performance metrics closely matching those of the molecular sensor in solution, directly overcoming the fatal flaw of functional degradation upon immobilization.

ideas.title: Nonlinear Optical (NLO) Sensing Platform for Label-Free, Real-Time Discrimination of Organic Anions
ideas.description: This research direction involves developing rubyrin-based thin films or surface-immobilized constructs as a nonlinear optical (NLO) sensing interface. The large, planar π-system of rubyrin provides a strong SHG signal, which will be characterized before and during exposure to a diverse panel of biologically relevant organic anions (e.g., citrate, succinate, lactate, pyruvate). The core hypothesis is that anion binding will alter the electron density and symmetry of the rubyrin core in anion-specific ways, producing distinct, quantifiable changes in the SHG intensity, polarization, or phase. This approach moves beyond simple analyte detection to enable real-time, label-free discrimination between structurally similar anions in aqueous or biological media based on their unique NLO 'fingerprint'.

ideas.title: Supramolecularly Imprinted Polymers for High-Fidelity Discrimination of Structurally Similar Organic Anions
ideas.description: This research direction combines supramolecular imprinting with broad-spectrum organic anion testing to create a library of highly selective synthetic receptors. Molecular imprinting will be performed using a diverse panel of biologically relevant organic anions (e.g., citrate, succinate, lactate, pyruvate, formate) as templates, creating polymers with cavities tailored to each anion's unique 3D geometry and functional group arrangement. The key innovation is the systematic cross-evaluation of each imprinted polymer against the entire panel, quantifying selectivity and revealing the structural features most critical for discrimination. This approach will generate fundamental design rules for creating synthetic anion receptors with antibody-like specificity for complex analytes, advancing applications in metabolic sensing and diagnostic assays.

ideas.title: Study electronic communication between asymmetric subunits

ideas.title: Inverse problem: design cation-selective version

ideas.title: Synthesize Janus dimers with different linker units

ideas.title: Create a dimeric or oligomeric corrphycen system
ideas.description: Link multiple corrphycen units covalently to study electronic communication.

ideas.title: Electronic Tongue for Anion Discrimination via Cross-Reactive Sensor Array
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'electronic tongue' concept from analytical chemistry to create a novel sensor array for anion pattern recognition. The core idea is to move beyond designing a single, perfectly selective sensor for each anion—a challenging task—and instead employ an array of broadly responsive, slightly different sensors. Inspired by how mammalian taste buds use broadly tuned receptors whose combined signals are interpreted by the brain, the array will consist of a small set (5-8) of electrochemical or optical sensors. Each sensor element will be functionalized with a different, partially selective receptor material (e.g., a suite of metalloporphyrins with varying metal centers, modified ionophores, or dye-displacement ensembles). When exposed to a sample, the array produces a unique, multi-dimensional response pattern or 'fingerprint' for each anion. This pattern is then analyzed using multivariate calibration or machine learning techniques (like principal component analysis or support vector machines) to both identify the anion present and estimate its concentration. This approach leverages the scaling of sensor diversity (SCALING_DUAL) to transform low-specificity individual responses into a high-specificity collective identification system, enabling simultaneous detection of multiple anions in environmental or biological samples.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: design a metal complex first

ideas.title: Metal coordination is irreversible or static
ideas.description: Develop Janus dimers with labile metal sites for dynamic or stimuli-responsive coordination.

ideas.title: Use materials science deposition methods
ideas.description: Employ Langmuir-Blodgett or vapor deposition to create thin films of expanded porphyrins for device integration.

ideas.title: Quantifying Rubyrin's Aromaticity via a Protein Folding Stability Analysis Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to quantifying protein folding stability from computational biochemistry to the problem of quantifying aromaticity in the planar macrocycle rubyrin. In protein studies, a composite picture of stability is built by integrating diverse computational and experimental metrics. Analogously, we propose to develop a multi-faceted, quantitative framework for rubyrin's aromaticity. This involves performing a coordinated suite of computational studies (DFT calculations for NICS scans, ACID analyses, HOMA, and ring current effects) alongside targeted experiments (NMR, magnetochemistry, electrochemistry). The data from these orthogonal techniques will be synthesized into a unified, weighted 'Aromaticity Stability Score' (ASS), providing a robust, holistic, and quantitative descriptor of rubyrin's electronic structure and aromatic character, moving beyond qualitative or single-metric descriptions.

ideas.title: Scale up to polymeric anion-exchange materials
ideas.description: Incorporate the macrocyclic unit into a porous polymer network for solid-phase anion capture.
ideas.sourceTitle: Ein nichtaromatisches, von Anthracen abgeleitetes expandiertes Porphyrin — ein Makrocyclus, der Anionen bindet

ideas.title: Protonation-State-Controlled Non-Planar Aromaticity in Rubyrin Scaffolds
ideas.description: This research direction involves the synthesis of deliberately non-planar, twisted rubyrin analogs designed to incorporate sites for variable protonation. By systematically exploring the deprotonated, monoprotonated, diprotonated, and potentially multiply protonated states of these distorted macrocycles, we will test how protonation-induced changes in electron count and charge distribution can either counteract or amplify the effects of structural non-planarity on aromaticity. This approach enables the decoupling and explicit study of the electronic (Hückel rule compliance) and geometric (planarity) contributions to aromatic stabilization. The ultimate goal is to discover if protonation can 'rescue' aromaticity in a non-planar framework or induce novel switching behavior between aromatic and non-aromatic states.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Affinity Maturation for Supramolecular Imprinting
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the analogous problem of generating high-affinity antibodies from the field of immunology and antibody engineering. The analogous solution uses directed evolution (e.g., phage display) to create diverse libraries and perform iterative selection for optimal antigen binding. Adapted to the research context of supramolecular imprinting for anions, this involves applying a combinatorial and evolutionary approach to MIP synthesis. A library of potential imprinting formulations is created, and iterative selection cycles based on anion binding performance are used to identify and evolve the polymeric compositions that produce the most effective synthetic binding pockets, thereby optimizing imprinting methodology beyond single, rationally designed attempts.

ideas.title: Synthesize contracted Turcasarin analogs
ideas.description: Create smaller macrocycles using similar building blocks but with fewer pyrrole units, like six or eight.
ideas.sourceTitle: Turcasarin, das bisher am stärksten expandierte Porphyrin

ideas.title: Dual: Janus dimer as a molecular diode
ideas.description: Exploit the electronic asymmetry for directional charge transport in a single molecule.

ideas.title: Functionally-Gated Combinatorial Discovery of Deployable Optical Sensors
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach applies high-throughput combinatorial chemistry to pyrrole and quinoxaline scaffolds but gates advancement on multi-parameter functional performance from the outset. Each compound in the library is screened not just for analyte response, but also for essential deployability criteria like solubility in target media, binding reversibility, and selectivity in a challenge matrix. This integrated screening funnel ensures that optimization drives the discovery of practically useful leads, not just potent binders. The final output is a shortlist of sensor candidates pre-validated for key real-world application hurdles, dramatically increasing the translation rate from discovery to prototype device.

ideas.title: Quantify Kinetic Penalty of Induced Fit to Engineer Flexible Anion Receptors with Practical Binding Rates
ideas.description: This project directly confronts the kinetic trade-off inherent in flexible anion receptors by designing a series of structurally related hosts where the degree of pre-organization is a continuous variable. The binding affinity, selectivity, and—critically—the association/dissociation rates for a panel of anions are measured in parallel. The goal is to identify the structural features in flexible systems that enable rapid conformational convergence to a selective binding geometry, thereby achieving high selectivity without a prohibitive kinetic cost. The output is a set of design rules for 'kinetically competent' induced-fit receptors.

ideas.title: Apply supramolecular chemistry host-guest methods
ideas.description: Use rubyrin's large cavity as a host for fullerenes or large anions from supramolecular chemistry.

ideas.title: Use computational chemistry for property prediction
ideas.description: Perform Hückel or DFT calculations to predict rubyrin's electronic spectrum and aromaticity indices.

ideas.title: Dual: use assembly for anion sequestration
ideas.description: Employ the self-assembled dimers as reversible traps for specific anions in solution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Bindung von Anionen: Selbstorganisation von Polypyrrolmakrocyclen†

ideas.title: Apply enzyme active site mimicry to optimize rubyrin-fullerene binding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from enzymology and protein engineering, where enzyme active sites are engineered with specific functional groups to achieve high-affinity, selective substrate binding. The analogous solution involves designing complementary non-covalent interactions within a binding pocket. Adapted to supramolecular chemistry, this means moving beyond simply using rubyrin's large cavity as a passive host. Instead, the rubyrin structure would be synthetically modified—by adding functional groups to its pyrrole units, meso-positions, or appended aromatic rings—to create a tailored interaction environment within its cavity. This engineered 'active site' would be designed to form optimal van der Waals, π-π, electrostatic, or hydrogen-bonding interactions with a specific target, like a fullerene (C60 or C70) or a large polyatomic anion (e.g., PF6-, BArF4-), thereby significantly enhancing binding affinity and selectivity over other potential guests.

ideas.title: Use computational modeling to predict dimer properties
ideas.description: Apply molecular orbital theory or DFT to model electronic structure and coupling before synthesis.

ideas.title: Test receptor for bicarbonate antiport activity
ideas.description: Directly evaluate the compound's proposed function as a chloride/bicarbonate antiport agent in lipid bilayers or vesicles.

ideas.title: Invert the anion detection paradigm
ideas.description: Design sensors where anion binding turns OFF the colorimetric/redox signal instead of turning it ON.

ideas.title: Anion Spectroscopy for Quinone Electronic State Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of absorption spectroscopy from astrophysics to materials chemistry. In astrophysics, unknown stellar properties are deduced by analyzing absorption lines from known interstellar elements. Here, the electronic state of quinone-containing materials (the unknown 'star') will be probed by observing their interaction with a series of known target anions (the 'interstellar probes'). The solution is to design an experiment where the quinone material's response—measured via techniques like cyclic voltammetry, UV-Vis, or impedance spectroscopy—is recorded upon systematic titration with different anions. The resulting dataset of 'anion-response fingerprints' will be inverted via computational modeling to map the underlying electronic state distribution, redox activity, and binding site heterogeneity within the material, effectively using anions as sensors to perform spectroscopic mapping of electronic structure.

ideas.title: Create polymeric membrane with embedded receptors
ideas.description: Copolymerize receptor derivatives to form solid-state anion-selective membranes for sensing or electrodialysis.

ideas.title: Dimers require direct covalent linkage
ideas.description: Construct Janus systems via supramolecular assembly or through-space interactions instead of covalent bonds.

ideas.description: Apply diffusion-ordered NMR spectroscopy to determine hydrodynamic radii of the assemblies.

ideas.title: Fabricate Metal-Coordinated Rubyrin Thin-Film Devices for Tunable Electronic and Sensing Applications
ideas.description: This research direction combines the metal-coordination chemistry of rubyrin with advanced polymer thin-film fabrication techniques. The core concept is to synthesize rubyrin-metal complexes (e.g., with Cu(II), Zn(II), or lanthanides) and then incorporate these pre-coordinated complexes into conductive polymer matrices or Langmuir-Blodgett films to create structured thin-film devices. The synergy lies in using the metal ion as a precise, built-in knob to tune the film's conductivity, redox potential, and selective analyte affinity, which are then leveraged in a solid-state device architecture. This enables the development of arrays of films with distinct, metal-defined properties for applications in multiplexed chemical sensing, tunable organic electronics, or spin-based devices, moving beyond simple film formation or solution-phase coordination studies alone.

ideas.title: Computationally Guided Design and Synthesis of Non-Centrosymmetric Rubyrin Derivatives for Second-Harmonic Generation
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea directly addresses the symmetry prohibition of the original concept. First, density functional theory (DFT) calculations are used to predict the first hyperpolarizability (β) of rubyrin and to design structurally asymmetric derivatives with push-pull substituents or asymmetric metal coordination. Targeted synthesis then produces these non-centrosymmetric chromophores. Their nonlinear optical response is rigorously quantified using solution-phase Hyper-Rayleigh Scattering to isolate molecular β values, followed by incorporation into poled polymeric films or crystalline assemblies to achieve the necessary non-centrosymmetric bulk order for solid-state SHG device evaluation.

ideas.description: Incorporate the dipyrrolylquinoxaline-quinone motif into solid supports or polymers for reusable devices.

ideas.description: Detect and characterize transient radical intermediates formed during electron transfer.

ideas.title: Use molecular dynamics for transport modeling
ideas.description: Simulate the macrocycle in a lipid bilayer to predict its anion transport pathway and efficiency.
